title: Data-Driven Management: Statistics for Managers
text:
Preface
This book introduces statistical reasoning to students and practitioners of management. It is designed for readers with no prior statistics background. Each chapter builds on the last, moving from description to probability to inference, and ends with interpretation — the step that most general-purpose statistics texts underserve.
The companion website, lecture slide decks, worked examples, and datasets are all part of the same project and are cross-referenced throughout.

title: About This Book
text:
How the book is organised
Data-Driven Management: Statistics for Managers was written for students who will use statistics to inform decisions, not to become statisticians. The emphasis throughout is on understanding what a result means and what to do with it.
The book moves in eight parts:

title: About This Book
text:
Foundations — what statistics is, how data is structured, and how samples relate to populations.
Describing Data — summarising and visualising distributions.
Probability — the logic that connects samples to populations.
Relationships — correlation and regression.
Estimation — confidence intervals and the central limit theorem.
Hypothesis Testing Logic — the framework shared by all inferential tests.
Core Tests — the most commonly used procedures.
Interpretation and Decision Use — choosing the right test and reading output correctly.

title: About This Book
text:
Datasets and software
All examples use R. Datasets are available at . Each chapter uses a named dataset drawn from realistic management scenarios.

title: About This Book
text:
For instructors
A full instructor resource set — including lesson plans, Moodle quiz bundles, and lecture slide decks — accompanies each chapter. Access is available at .

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:

text:

Explain why managers rely on data rather than intuition alone.
Describe the basic cycle that connects a question to a statistical answer.
Identify realistic management situations in which statistical thinking applies.
Recognise the limits of what statistics can and cannot do.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.2 Introduction
Every manager makes decisions under uncertainty. A retailer does not know how many customers will arrive on a given Saturday. A hospital administrator cannot predict how many beds will be required next month. A product manager launching a new feature has no means of reading the market in advance. What statistics offers is not certainty. It offers a disciplined way of reducing uncertainty and of measuring what remains.
This chapter addresses a basic question: what is statistics, and why does it matter to a person whose work is to manage an organisation rather than to study numbers for their own sake? The answer has two parts. First, statistics is a set of tools for collecting, summarising, and drawing conclusions from data. Second, and more important for the practitioner, it is a way of thinking, one that separates what the evidence actually shows from what one assumes or hopes it shows.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.3 What statistics is
The word statistics is used in at least three senses. It refers to a discipline, a field of scholarly inquiry. It refers to methods, the procedures employed to collect and analyse data. And it refers to numbers themselves, particularly numbers that summarise a dataset, such as an average or a percentage.
All three senses are relevant, but the most useful definition for a working manager is this: statistics is the science of learning from data under conditions of uncertainty.
The phrase under conditions of uncertainty is what distinguishes statistics from arithmetic. Adding up this quarter’s sales figures is arithmetic. Deciding whether those figures convey something reliable about next quarter’s demand is a statistical question.

1.3.1 The two branches
Statistics divides into two broad areas: descriptive statistics and inferential statistics.
Descriptive statistics summarises and organises data that one already possesses. Calculating the average customer satisfaction score across 500 survey responses, or drawing a bar chart showing sales by region, are both descriptive activities. Nothing is being predicted or inferred. Existing information is simply placed in a form that is easier to comprehend.
Inferential statistics goes further. It uses data from a smaller group, a sample, to draw conclusions about a larger group, a population. If 200 customers are surveyed and those results are used to estimate how all customers feel, an inference is being made. That inference involves uncertainty, and statistics provides the tools to measure and communicate how much uncertainty is present.
The distinction matters in practice. Descriptive statistics reports what occurred in the data one holds. Inferential statistics assists in judging what is probably true more broadly, and how confident one should be in that judgement.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.4 Why managers need statistical thinking
Managers have always worked with numbers. What has changed is the volume of data available and the expectations placed on decisions. Spreadsheets, customer databases, website analytics, and operational systems now produce more data than any individual can read. The question is no longer whether data are available. It is whether those data can be interpreted with honesty and rigour.

1.4.1 The problem with intuition
Human intuition is well suited to many activities: reading people, navigating social situations, making rapid judgements in familiar contexts. It is poorly suited to detecting patterns in large datasets, estimating probabilities, and recognising when a trend is real rather than accidental.
Several well-documented biases affect how people naturally interpret data, as discussed at length in Kahneman (2011).
Availability bias leads one to overweight information that comes to mind readily. A dramatic customer complaint, for instance, tends to receive disproportionate attention relative to the broader pattern of feedback.
Confirmation bias leads one to attend more closely to evidence that supports existing beliefs and to discount evidence that challenges them.
Small-sample thinking leads one to treat a small number of observations as more informative than they actually are. If the last three product launches succeeded, confidence in the fourth tends to be high, even when three observations are nowhere near sufficient to establish a reliable pattern.
Statistical thinking does not eliminate these biases. It provides a framework for checking conclusions against evidence, rather than allowing evidence to be bent to fit conclusions.


1.4.2 What managers actually do with statistics
The applications of statistics in management are not abstract. They appear in routine work across every organisational function, as Anderson et al. (2020) illustrates with extensive business case material.
Operations and logistics. A distribution centre manager uses historical data on delivery times to set realistic expectations and to detect deterioration in performance before it becomes a crisis.
Marketing. A marketing team tests two versions of an email subject line, sending each to half of a randomly selected list. A statistical test determines whether the difference in open rates reflects a real difference or merely random variation.
Finance. A finance analyst reviewing branch performance needs to know whether one branch’s lower profit margin reflects a genuine structural problem or simply the short duration of the comparison period.
Human resources. An HR director examining pay data across the organisation wants to determine whether apparent differences in pay between groups reflect experience and role, or whether they indicate something that warrants further investigation.
In each case, the statistical question has the same underlying structure: is what is visible in the data a reliable signal, or is it noise?
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
section: 1.6 The basic cycle: from question to answer
text:
1.6 The basic cycle: from question to answer
Statistical work tends to follow a recognisable sequence. Understanding this sequence helps managers commission and evaluate analysis, even when they are not conducting the calculations themselves.

1.6.1 Step 1: Define the question
Good statistical work begins with a clear, specific question. “How are sales?” is not a statistical question. “Did average weekly sales in the northern region increase between the first and second quarter of this financial year, and if so, by how much?” is one. The specificity matters because vague questions produce unfocused analysis that is difficult to interpret and easy to misuse.


1.6.2 Step 2: Collect data
Once the question is established, data that can actually answer it must be obtained. This involves decisions about what to measure, where to collect information, how to record it, and how to ensure accuracy. Poor data collection undermines everything that follows. A statistically sophisticated analysis of badly collected data produces confident but incorrect answers.


1.6.3 Step 3: Describe the data
Before drawing any conclusions, one must examine what is available. Summary statistics and visualisations such as histograms and scatter plots reveal the basic shape of the data. This step frequently brings to light errors, outliers, or patterns that alter the direction of the analysis.


1.6.4 Step 4: Analyse and draw conclusions
Depending on the question, this step may involve calculating a confidence interval, running a hypothesis test, or building a regression model. The output is not a final answer but a probabilistic statement: given these data, here is what the evidence supports, and here is how certain one can be.


1.6.5 Step 5: Communicate and decide
Statistical conclusions must be translated into plain language before they can be useful to decision-makers. A p-value conveys little to most managers. What they need to know is whether the evidence is strong enough to act on, what the margin of error is, and what would follow if the conclusion proved to be incorrect.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.7 What statistics cannot do
It is worth being precise about the limits of statistics, because unrealistic expectations lead either to over-reliance on the method or to cynicism about it.
Statistics does not prove things. It assesses the weight of evidence. A well-designed study that produces a strong result gives good reason to believe something. It does not make that something certain.
Statistics does not remove the need for judgement. A statistical test may indicate that the difference between two approaches is statistically significant. It cannot indicate whether that difference is large enough to matter in practice, or whether the cost of acting on it is justified.
Statistics cannot correct for bad data. If the data collected do not measure what was intended, or if the sample was not representative of the population of interest, no degree of analytical sophistication will rescue the result.
Statistics does not establish causation on its own. A correlation between two variables does not by itself establish that one caused the other. Causation requires additional evidence beyond the correlation alone.
These limits are not reasons to distrust statistics. They are reasons to use it carefully, to read results critically, and to treat statistical conclusions as one important input among several, rather than as the final word.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.8 Worked example






Note



Scenario. A regional manager at a coffee chain observes that three branches in her area reported fewer customer complaints last month than in the previous month. She concludes that the service improvement initiative launched six weeks earlier is working, and recommends extending it to all 47 branches.


This chain of reasoning is understandable but contains several problems that statistical thinking would identify.
Problem 1: Sample size. Three branches is a very small number. Random variation alone can easily produce a month in which complaints happen to be lower in three out of forty-seven locations.
Problem 2: No comparison group. If complaints fell across all branches rather than only in the three that received the initiative, something else may explain the decline. Perhaps it was a quieter month overall.
Problem 3: Causation assumed from correlation. Even if complaints genuinely fell in the three branches, one cannot conclude that the initiative caused the fall without ruling out other explanations.
A more defensible approach would be to compare complaint rates across all branches, assess whether the three treated branches differ from the rest, and conduct a formal test before drawing conclusions. Chapters 20 through 23 cover the tools required for exactly this kind of analysis.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.9 Summary
Statistics is the science of learning from data under conditions of uncertainty. It divides into descriptive statistics, which summarises data already collected, and inferential statistics, which uses sample data to draw conclusions about a broader population.
Managers require statistical thinking because human intuition is unreliable when dealing with large datasets, probability, and patterns that may be real or may be random. Every business function regularly faces questions that can only be answered well with data and a framework for interpreting it honestly.
The basic cycle of statistical work runs from defining a clear question, through collecting and describing data, through analysis, to communication and decision. Statistics does not produce certainty, does not remove the need for judgement, and cannot correct bad data or establish causation on its own.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It

1.10 Key terms



Table 1.2: Key terms for Chapter 01


Term
Definition




Statistics
The science of learning from data under conditions of uncertainty.


Descriptive statistics
Methods for summarising and organising data that have already been collected.


Inferential statistics
Methods for using sample data to draw conclusions about a population.


Population
The complete group of individuals or cases that a study concerns.


Sample
A subset of the population, selected to represent it.


Data
Recorded observations or measurements, organised in variables and cases.


Availability bias
The tendency to overweight information that comes readily to mind.


Confirmation bias
The tendency to favour evidence that supports existing beliefs.

title: 1  Chapter 01: What Statistics Is and Why Managers Use It
text:
1.11 Exercises

A colleague says: “We received three complaints about the new product last week, so something is clearly wrong.” What statistical concern does this statement raise, and how would you respond?
Identify one decision made in the past month at an organisation with which you are familiar. Was data used? If so, was the analysis descriptive, inferential, or both? If data were not used, what information would have assisted the decision?
A report states: “Regions that spent more on training had higher sales.” A manager concludes that training causes higher sales. What is wrong with this conclusion, and what additional evidence would be required to support it?
List three questions from your own field or area of interest that could be addressed statistically. For each, note whether the question is primarily descriptive or inferential.
A chief executive reads a summary reporting that 78 per cent of employees are “satisfied” with their work. She says: “Good, most people are happy.” What questions should she ask before accepting this conclusion?

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, students should be able to:
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
Distinguish clearly between descriptive and inferential statistics.
Describe the role of each branch in a complete analysis.
Recognise which branch is being used in a given report or summary.
Explain why moving from description to inference introduces uncertainty.
Identify common errors that arise from treating descriptive results as if they were inferential conclusions.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.2 Introduction
The distinction between descriptive and inferential statistics is one of the most important in the field, and also one of the most frequently blurred in practice. Reports that describe what happened in a dataset are often written as though they prove something about the wider world. Summaries of a sample are presented as facts about a population. The move from “here is what we found in our data” to “here is what is probably true more broadly” is a significant one, and it requires a different set of tools, as well as a different set of caveats.
This chapter draws that line carefully. Both branches are necessary. Neither is sufficient on its own.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.3 Descriptive statistics
Descriptive statistics concerns itself with what is actually in front of you. It covers methods for organising, summarising, and presenting data so that it can be understood without having to read every individual observation.
Suppose a company has collected weekly sales figures from all 30 of its stores over the past year. That is 1,560 individual numbers. No one reads 1,560 numbers and comes away with a clear picture. But a table showing average weekly sales by store, a chart displaying how sales changed over the year, and a single number capturing the spread between the best and worst performers, and these make the data interpretable.
Descriptive statistics includes:

Measures of centre: mean, median, mode.
Measures of spread: range, variance, standard deviation, interquartile range.
Frequency distributions and histograms.
Graphs for categorical and numerical data.
Cross-tabulations and summary tables.


2.3.1 A critical point about descriptive statistics
Descriptive statistics makes no claims beyond the data at hand. If the 30 stores are all the stores the company owns, then the average you calculate is not an estimate. It is the actual average, computed from every relevant case. There is no sampling error, no margin of uncertainty, no inference involved. You are describing a complete picture.
This matters because it defines the limits of the descriptive approach. The moment you want to say something about cases not in your data, including future customers, stores not yet open, the broader market, you have moved into inferential territory.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.4 Inferential statistics
Inferential statistics uses data from a sample to draw conclusions about a population. The word infer is exact: you are reasoning from what you can see to what you cannot see directly.
This step introduces uncertainty, and that uncertainty is not a weakness of the method. It is an honest acknowledgement of reality. You did not measure everyone. You measured some people. The gap between what you measured and what you want to know is the source of the uncertainty, and inferential statistics provides the tools to quantify it.
The main tools of inferential statistics are:

Estimation: using sample statistics to estimate population parameters, typically with a confidence interval that communicates the precision of the estimate.
Hypothesis testing: using sample data to evaluate a specific claim about a population, for instance whether a new drug works better than the old one, whether two groups differ on some measure, whether a trend is real or coincidental.


2.4.1 Why inference is necessary
Managers almost never have access to complete population data. A hotel chain wanting to know how guests feel cannot interview every person who has ever stayed. A pharmaceutical company testing a new drug cannot give it to every patient who might someday use it. A manufacturer checking product quality cannot inspect every item off the line.
In each case, a sample is taken, and the results of that sample are used to say something about the population. Done properly, this is not a compromise. Done properly, this is efficient, and the uncertainty it introduces can be measured and communicated honestly.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.5 The two branches working together
In practice, a good analysis typically uses both branches in sequence. You begin by describing your data, computing summaries, drawing graphs, looking for patterns. This is exploratory and often reveals things that change the direction of the analysis. Then, once you have a clear sense of what the data contain, you use inferential tools to ask whether what you found is likely to reflect something real or whether it might be a product of random variation in your sample.
Consider a worked scenario. A supermarket chain has trialled a new store layout in 12 of its 200 stores over a three-month period. At the end of the trial, they compare average transaction values in the 12 trial stores against the 188 stores that kept the old layout.
The descriptive step: calculate the mean transaction value for each group, display the distributions, look for any obvious differences in the data.
The inferential step: use a two-sample t-test (covered in Chapter 23) to determine whether the observed difference in means is large enough to be unlikely under the assumption that the layout made no difference.
The descriptive step tells you what the data look like. The inferential step tells you how seriously to take what you see.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.6 Common errors at the boundary

2.6.1 Treating a sample summary as a population fact
Suppose a company surveys 150 of its customers and finds that 62% prefer the new product design. A manager announces: “62% of our customers prefer the new design.” This statement treats an estimate as a fact. The true proportion is unknown. The sample gives an estimate, which could be stated more honestly as: “Based on 150 responses, we estimate that between 54% and 70% of customers prefer the new design”, with the range reflecting the uncertainty in the estimate.


2.6.2 Applying population data inferentially when description is sufficient
The reverse error also occurs. If you have collected data from every employee in your organisation and you want to describe something about those employees, a formal hypothesis test is not needed. The data is the population. There is nothing to infer.
This mistake tends to produce a kind of false rigour: running a t-test on data that already represents the whole group, and then reporting that the result was “statistically significant” when significance only applies to inference from samples.


2.6.3 Overgeneralising from a convenient sample
A sample collected from whoever was easiest to reach, for instance customers who filled out a feedback form voluntarily, for instance, is not representative of all customers. Inferential conclusions drawn from such a sample will be biased, often in ways that are hard to detect from the data alone.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.7 Worked example


Code
# Descriptive vs inferential, a simple illustration
# 
# Scenario: A consulting firm tracked billable hours for a team of 8 analysts
# over the past month. These are the actual hours for all 8 analysts. These are
# IS the population (for this team, this month).

billable_hours <- c(142, 158, 135, 167, 149, 153, 140, 161)

# Descriptive: describe what we have
cat("Team descriptive summary\n")


Team descriptive summary


Code
cat("------------------------\n")


------------------------


Code
cat("Mean:   ", round(mean(billable_hours), 1), "hours\n")


Mean:    150.6 hours


Code
cat("Median: ", median(billable_hours), "hours\n")


Median:  151 hours


Code
cat("SD:     ", round(sd(billable_hours), 1), "hours\n")


SD:      11.1 hours


Code
cat("Min:    ", min(billable_hours), "hours\n")


Min:     135 hours


Code
cat("Max:    ", max(billable_hours), "hours\n")


Max:     167 hours


Because these are the actual hours for the entire team, the mean of 150.6 hours is not an estimate. It is the true team average for that month. Descriptive statistics is all that is needed.


Code
# If instead we had data from only 8 analysts drawn from a firm of 300,
# and we wanted to estimate the firm-wide average, we would need inference.
# The sample mean becomes an estimate, and we would report it with a
# confidence interval (covered fully in Chapter 18).

n      <- length(billable_hours)
x_bar  <- mean(billable_hours)
se     <- sd(billable_hours) / sqrt(n)
t_crit <- qt(0.975, df = n - 1)

lower <- x_bar - t_crit * se
upper <- x_bar + t_crit * se

cat(sprintf("95%% confidence interval for firm-wide mean: [%.1f, %.1f] hours\n",
            lower, upper))


95% confidence interval for firm-wide mean: [141.3, 159.9] hours


Code
cat("(If these 8 were a random sample from a larger firm)\n")


(If these 8 were a random sample from a larger firm)


The same numbers, used differently, produce two different kinds of statement. The descriptive statement is exact. The inferential statement is probabilistic and comes with a range.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.8 Summary
Descriptive statistics summarises data that has been collected, making no claim beyond what the data directly contains. Inferential statistics uses sample data to draw conclusions about a larger population, introducing quantifiable uncertainty.
Both branches are necessary in practice. Description comes first and reveals what the data contain. Inference follows and asks how much confidence to place in what was found. The most common errors at this boundary involve presenting descriptive results as though they were established population facts, or running inferential tests on data that already cover the full population.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.9 Key terms



Table 02.2: Key terms for Chapter 02


Term
Definition




Descriptive statistics
Methods for summarising and organising data that have already been collected.


Inferential statistics
Methods for using sample data to draw conclusions about a population.


Parameter
A numerical characteristic of a population, such as the population mean.


Statistic
A numerical characteristic of a sample, used to estimate a population parameter.


Estimation
Using sample data to approximate the value of a population parameter.


Hypothesis test
A procedure for evaluating a specific claim about a population using sample data.


Confidence interval
A range of values within which the true parameter is estimated to lie, with a stated probability.


Selection bias
Systematic error arising from a sample that is not representative of the population.
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics
text:
2.10 Exercises

A city council surveys 400 residents about satisfaction with local transport. The report states: “68% of residents are satisfied with local transport.” Is this a descriptive or an inferential statement? What would need to change to make it a properly qualified inferential claim?
A department head has performance ratings for all 22 people in her team. She runs a t-test comparing the ratings of employees hired in the last two years versus those hired more than two years ago. What error is she making, and how should she approach the comparison instead?
A national retailer analyses purchasing data from 50,000 loyalty card holders. Is this a sample or a population? Explain your reasoning. Does it matter for how the results should be reported?
In your own words, explain why inferential statistics necessarily involves uncertainty, while descriptive statistics (applied to a complete dataset) does not.
A report concludes: “Customers who received the loyalty discount spent an average of £42 per visit, compared to £38 for those who did not, a statistically significant difference.” What additional information would you need before deciding whether the loyalty discount is actually driving higher spending?
crumbs: 2  Chapter 02: Descriptive and Inferential Statistics

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, students should be able to:
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
Define population, sample, parameter, and statistic precisely.
Explain why samples rather than populations are usually studied.
Define sampling error and explain why it is unavoidable.
Describe how sample size affects the reliability of an estimate.
Distinguish sampling error from non-sampling error.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.2 Introduction
Before any statistical analysis can be interpreted, four terms need to be understood exactly: population, sample, parameter, and statistic. These words are used loosely in everyday conversation, “the population of users who clicked the link,” “a statistic showing customer satisfaction”, but in statistics they have precise meanings that determine what kind of claim you are entitled to make.
This chapter defines each term, explains why the distinction between them matters, and introduces sampling error: the unavoidable gap between what a sample shows and what the population actually looks like.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.3 Population
In statistics, a population is the complete set of individuals, objects, or events that a study is concerned with. The definition of the population comes from the research question, not from any property of the data collected.
Some examples clarify the range:

A study of customer satisfaction at a retail chain might define the population as all customers who made a purchase in the last 12 months.
A quality control analysis might define the population as all units produced by a particular machine on a particular shift.
A study of graduate outcomes might define the population as all students who graduated from a given programme over a ten-year period.

Populations can be finite (a fixed number of members) or infinite (ongoing processes, future events, all possible measurements under a given condition). They can be large or small. What makes them the population is that the research question is about them, they are the group to which the conclusions are intended to apply.
This definition has an important consequence. If you collect data from every member of your population, you have a census. A census is rare. Most populations are too large, too dispersed, or too dynamic to measure completely, which is why most statistical work relies on samples.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.4 Sample
A sample is a subset of the population, selected for actual observation or measurement. The sample is what you have; the population is what you want to know about.
The value of a sample depends on how well it represents the population. A sample of 500 customers drawn randomly from your complete customer database is far more useful than a sample of 500 customers who happened to fill in a feedback card on a particular Tuesday, even if both samples are the same size. The first sample is likely to represent the full range of customers. The second is likely to over-represent certain types.
How the sample is selected, the sampling method, is one of the most consequential decisions in any study. Chapter 4 covers this in detail.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.5 Parameters and statistics
The terms parameter and statistic describe numbers that summarise a group, but which group matters.
A parameter is a numerical summary of a population. The mean income of all adults in a country, the proportion of all products from a factory that are defective, the standard deviation of all exam scores from an entire cohort, these are parameters. Parameters are usually unknown, because the complete population data is usually unavailable.
A statistic (singular) is a numerical summary of a sample. The mean income of 1,200 adults surveyed, the proportion of defective products in a batch of 200 inspected items, the standard deviation of exam scores from 150 students sampled from a cohort, these are statistics. Statistics are known, because they are calculated directly from the data collected.
The goal of inferential statistics is to use statistics to estimate parameters. The sample mean estimates the population mean. The sample proportion estimates the population proportion. Because the sample is only a subset of the population, the estimate will not be exact, and that gap has a name.

3.5.1 Notation convention
A standard notation makes the distinction concrete:




Population (parameter)
Sample (statistic)




Mean
μ (mu)
x̄ (x-bar)


Standard deviation
σ (sigma)
s


Proportion
π or p
p̂ (p-hat)


Size
N
n



Parameters are typically denoted by Greek letters. Sample statistics use Roman letters or letters with a hat (^). When you see μ, you are being told something about a population. When you see x̄, you are being told something about a sample.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.6 Sampling error
Sampling error is the difference between a sample statistic and the corresponding population parameter. It arises not from mistakes in measurement or calculation but from the simple fact that a sample is not the whole population.
If the average weekly revenue across all 200 branches of a retail chain is £48,200 (the parameter μ), but a random sample of 30 branches yields an average of £47,650 (the statistic x̄), the sampling error is £48,200 − £47,650 = £550. No one made an error. The sample just happened to include slightly lower-revenue branches than the full population average would suggest.
This matters in three ways:
Sampling error is unavoidable. Any sample will differ from the population to some degree. The goal is not to eliminate sampling error but to keep it manageable and to measure it honestly.
Sampling error varies with sample size. Larger samples tend to produce smaller sampling errors. This is because larger samples are less likely to deviate substantially from the population as a whole. The precise relationship is captured by the standard error, which is covered in Chapter 17.
Sampling error is random. If you drew many different samples from the same population, the statistics would vary around the true parameter. Some samples would over-estimate, some would under-estimate, but the average of many sample statistics would be close to the parameter. This property is the foundation of confidence intervals and hypothesis testing.

3.6.1 Sampling error versus non-sampling error
Sampling error should not be confused with other sources of inaccuracy, which are collected under the term non-sampling error. Non-sampling errors include:

Measurement error: the question was poorly worded, the instrument was miscalibrated, or responses were recorded incorrectly.
Coverage error: the sampling frame (the list from which the sample was drawn) did not include all members of the population, so certain groups had no chance of being selected.
Non-response bias: the people who agreed to participate differ systematically from those who did not.
Processing error: data entry or coding errors introduced after the data was collected.

Non-sampling errors, unlike sampling error, do not necessarily decrease as sample size grows. Surveying more people from a biased sampling frame gives you a larger biased sample, it does not make the sample more representative.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.7 Worked example


Code
set.seed(42)

# Simulate a population of 5,000 customer transaction values (in £)
# Population mean (parameter) is known here because we are constructing the
# population, in practice it would not be.
population <- rnorm(5000, mean = 85, sd = 22)
mu <- mean(population)

# Draw 500 samples of size n = 30 and record each sample mean
sample_means_30 <- replicate(500, mean(sample(population, 30)))

# Draw 500 samples of size n = 100
sample_means_100 <- replicate(500, mean(sample(population, 100)))

# Compare
data.frame(
  sample_mean = c(sample_means_30, sample_means_100),
  size = factor(rep(c("n = 30", "n = 100"), each = 500))
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = sample_mean, fill = size)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mu, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#C0392B", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#1B3A5C", "#E8A020")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Sampling error shrinks as sample size increases",
    subtitle = sprintf("True population mean (dashed line) = £%.2f", mu),
    x        = "Sample mean (£)",
    y        = "Count of samples",
    fill     = "Sample size",
    caption  = "500 samples drawn at each size from a simulated population of 5,000 transactions."
  ) +
  theme_dmm()









The chart shows that both distributions of sample means are centred near the true population mean, neither sample size produces systematically biased estimates. But the spread of sample means is considerably wider for n = 30 than for n = 100. Larger samples cluster more tightly around the true value. This is what it means to say that larger samples reduce sampling error.


Code
data.frame(
  "Sample size"  = c(30, 100),
  "Mean of sample means" = c(mean(sample_means_30), mean(sample_means_100)),
  "SD of sample means"   = c(sd(sample_means_30), sd(sample_means_100))
) |>
  dmm_table(caption = "Effect of sample size on sampling error (500 simulated samples each)", digits = 2)



Effect of sample size on sampling error (500 simulated samples each)


Sample.size
Mean.of.sample.means
SD.of.sample.means




30
84.89
4.03


100
84.65
2.14





The standard deviation of the sample means, called the standard error, falls substantially when the sample size quadruples from 30 to 100. This illustrates a key result: the standard error decreases in proportion to the square root of the sample size, not the sample size itself. To halve the sampling error, you need to quadruple the sample size.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.8 Summary
A population is the complete set of cases a study is concerned with. A sample is the subset actually observed. A parameter is a numerical summary of the population; a statistic is a numerical summary of the sample. Inferential statistics uses statistics to estimate parameters.
Sampling error is the inevitable difference between a sample statistic and the true population parameter. It arises from the randomness of the sampling process, not from mistakes. It decreases as sample size increases but never reaches zero unless a complete census is taken. Sampling error is distinct from non-sampling error, which can affect results regardless of sample size and which cannot be reduced simply by collecting more observations from the same flawed process.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.9 Key terms



Table 03.2: Key terms for Chapter 03


Term
Definition




Population
The complete set of individuals, objects, or events to which a study's conclusions are intended to apply.


Sample
A subset of the population, selected for actual observation or measurement.


Census
A study in which data are collected from every member of the population.


Parameter
A fixed numerical characteristic of a population.


Statistic
A numerical summary computed from sample data; used to estimate a parameter.


Sampling error
The difference between a sample statistic and the corresponding population parameter.


Standard error
The standard deviation of a sampling distribution; measures the precision of an estimate.


Non-sampling error
Error arising from sources other than sampling variability, such as measurement error or non-response.
crumbs: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error

title: 3  Chapter 03: Populations, Samples, Parameters, Statistics, and Sampling Error
text:
3.10 Exercises

A supermarket wants to know the average amount spent per visit by all customers last month. Define the population, state what a parameter would be in this context, and explain why a census would be impractical.
A sample of 50 employees is drawn from a workforce of 800. The sample has a mean age of 34.2 years. Is 34.2 a parameter or a statistic? What symbol would be used to represent it?
Two analysts estimate the same population mean using different sample sizes: one uses n = 40, the other uses n = 160. If both use random sampling, which estimate is likely to be closer to the true parameter, and why?
A company sends a customer satisfaction survey to 1,000 randomly selected customers, but only 120 respond. What type of error is the main concern here, and why can it not be reduced simply by sending the survey to more people?
In the worked example, why are both distributions of sample means centred near the true population mean of £85, even though one uses samples of only 30 observations?

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, students should be able to:

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
Explain what a sampling frame is and why it matters.
Describe simple random sampling and how to implement it.
Describe systematic, stratified, cluster, and convenience sampling.
Compare probability and non-probability sampling methods.
Identify the risk of bias in non-probability samples.
Select an appropriate sampling method for a given management scenario.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.2 Introduction
How you select a sample determines what you can legitimately say about the population. The most sophisticated statistical analysis cannot rescue a poorly drawn sample. A result derived from a biased sample is not an imprecise estimate, it is a systematically wrong one, and more data from the same source will not fix it.
This chapter covers the most common sampling methods, the conditions under which each is appropriate, and the errors that arise when the method does not match the question.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.3 The sampling frame
Before selecting a sample, you need a sampling frame: a list or organised representation of the population from which the sample will be drawn. If the population is all active customers, the sampling frame might be the customer database. If the population is all products manufactured last week, the frame might be the production log.
The sampling frame should cover the population completely. When it does not, coverage error results: some members of the population have no chance of being selected, which introduces systematic bias. A telephone survey conducted using landline numbers, for example, automatically excludes everyone who only has a mobile phone, a group that differs in potentially important ways from the rest of the population.
Identifying a complete and accurate sampling frame is often the most difficult practical challenge in data collection.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.4 Probability sampling methods
In probability sampling, every member of the population has a known, non-zero probability of being selected. This property is what makes inferential conclusions justifiable, without it, you cannot calculate sampling error or construct valid confidence intervals.

4.4.1 Simple random sampling
In simple random sampling (SRS), every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected, and every possible sample of size n has an equal chance of being the one drawn.
In practice, SRS is implemented by assigning a number to each member of the sampling frame and then using a random number generator (or statistical software) to select the sample. In R:


Code
set.seed(101)
population_ids <- 1:500   # a frame of 500 employees
sample_ids     <- sample(population_ids, size = 50)
head(sample_ids, 10)


 [1] 329 313 430  95 209 442 351 317 444 315


SRS is the conceptual baseline against which all other methods are compared. Its main limitation is practical: it requires a complete sampling frame, and when the population is large and geographically dispersed, reaching randomly selected members can be expensive or logistically difficult.


4.4.2 Systematic sampling
Systematic sampling selects every k-th member of the sampling frame, where k is the population size divided by the desired sample size. The starting point is chosen at random from the first k members.
If a frame contains 1,000 customers and a sample of 100 is needed, k = 10. A starting point between 1 and 10 is chosen at random, say, 7, and then every 10th member is selected: 7, 17, 27, 37, and so on.
Systematic sampling is convenient and produces results close to SRS when the frame has no periodic pattern. It becomes problematic if the frame is arranged in a cycle that aligns with the sampling interval. A list of store opening shifts ordered by day of the week, sampled every 7th entry, would consistently draw from the same day.


4.4.3 Stratified sampling
Stratified sampling divides the population into non-overlapping subgroups (strata) based on a characteristic relevant to the study, and then draws a separate random sample from each stratum.
The advantage is that it guarantees representation of each subgroup and can produce more precise estimates than SRS when the strata differ substantially from each other.
Proportionate stratified sampling draws from each stratum in proportion to its size in the population. If women make up 60% of the workforce, 60% of the sample comes from the female stratum.
Disproportionate stratified sampling deliberately over-samples smaller strata to ensure adequate representation. This is useful when you want to make comparisons between groups of very different sizes, for example, comparing satisfaction scores across three product lines where one line accounts for 80% of sales and another for only 5%.


Code
# Proportionate stratified sample from a workforce with three departments
set.seed(202)

workforce <- data.frame(
  id         = 1:600,
  department = rep(c("Operations", "Sales", "Finance"), times = c(300, 200, 100))
)

target_n <- 60  # total sample

# Sample 10% from each stratum
library(dplyr)
stratified_sample <- workforce |>
  group_by(department) |>
  slice_sample(prop = 0.10) |>
  ungroup()

table(stratified_sample$department)



   Finance Operations      Sales 
        10         30         20 




4.4.4 Cluster sampling
Cluster sampling divides the population into clusters, typically defined by geography or administrative groupings, selects a random sample of clusters, and then includes all (or a random sub-sample of) members within the chosen clusters.
A retailer wanting to survey customers across 120 stores might find it impractical to sample from all stores. Instead, they randomly select 20 stores and survey all customers who shop there during a defined period. The 20 stores are the clusters.
Cluster sampling is more economical than SRS for geographically dispersed populations but typically less statistically efficient: if people within clusters are similar to each other (as neighbours or regular customers of the same store often are), the effective sample diversity is lower than the headcount suggests.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.5 Non-probability sampling
In non-probability sampling, members of the population are not selected according to known, random probabilities. This makes it impossible to calculate sampling error formally, which limits what can legitimately be concluded.
Non-probability samples are widely used in practice, particularly in exploratory research, user testing, and settings where probability sampling is simply not feasible. They are not inherently worthless, but the conclusions drawn from them should be proportionately cautious.

4.5.1 Convenience sampling
A convenience sample includes whoever is easiest to reach: shoppers in a particular location, respondents who click on a survey link, students in a class. It is the most common type of non-probability sample and the one most vulnerable to bias.
The problem is not the method itself but the conclusions it is used to support. If a product team surveys a convenience sample of 200 users and concludes “our users prefer Feature A,” the honest statement is: “of the 200 people who responded to this survey, most preferred Feature A.” Whether that reflects the broader user base is unknown.


4.5.2 Purposive sampling
Purposive sampling (also called judgement sampling) selects participants based on the researcher’s judgement that they are representative or informative. An industry analyst interviewing senior executives from leading firms is using purposive sampling. It can be appropriate in qualitative research and when specific expertise is being sought, but it cannot support quantitative generalisations.


4.5.3 Snowball sampling
Snowball sampling starts with a small number of participants who then refer other potential participants. It is useful when the target population is hard to reach, such as members of a professional network or participants in niche markets, but the resulting sample is unlikely to be representative of the broader population.


4.5.4 Quota sampling
Quota sampling sets targets for the composition of the sample (similar to stratified sampling) but fills those targets non-randomly. An interviewer might be instructed to find 50 men and 50 women, but left to approach whoever is convenient. Because the selection within each quota is not random, the formal guarantees of stratified sampling do not apply.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.6 Choosing a sampling method
The choice of sampling method depends on several factors:







Factor
Consideration




Do you need formal statistical inference?
If yes, probability sampling is required.


Is a complete sampling frame available?
If no, probability sampling may not be possible.


How large and dispersed is the population?
Large/dispersed populations may require cluster or systematic approaches.


Are there important subgroups to represent?
Stratification may be advisable.


What resources are available?
Census and SRS can be expensive; cluster and systematic are more economical.


Is the research exploratory?
Convenience or purposive sampling may be acceptable if conclusions are qualified.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.7 Worked example






Note



Scenario. A regional health service wants to survey staff satisfaction across 40 hospitals of varying sizes. Total clinical staff: approximately 22,000.


Option 1, Simple random sampling. Assign a number to each of the 22,000 staff members and draw 1,100 (5%) randomly. Statistically ideal but operationally difficult: reaching specific named individuals across 40 institutions requires coordination across HR systems.
Option 2, Stratified sampling by hospital size. Divide the 40 hospitals into small, medium, and large strata. Draw a proportionate random sample from each stratum’s staff list. This ensures that the sample represents the range of institutional settings and allows comparisons between hospital sizes.
Option 3, Cluster sampling. Randomly select 10 of the 40 hospitals and survey all clinical staff within those 10. Less statistically efficient (staff within a hospital are likely to share similar experiences), but logistically far simpler.
For a survey intended to produce nationally generalisable estimates and allow subgroup comparisons, stratified sampling is the strongest choice. If budget constrains data collection to a few sites, cluster sampling with 10 randomly chosen hospitals is defensible, provided conclusions are qualified accordingly.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.8 Summary
A sampling frame is the list from which a sample is drawn; gaps in the frame produce coverage error. Probability sampling methods, simple random, systematic, stratified, and cluster, each give every population member a known chance of selection, which is what makes formal statistical inference possible. Non-probability methods, convenience, purposive, snowball, quota, are more common in practice but do not support formal inference because sampling probabilities are unknown.
The choice of sampling method should be made before data collection, guided by the research question, the available frame, practical constraints, and the conclusions the analysis is expected to support.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.9 Key terms



Table 04.2: Key terms for Chapter 04


Term
Definition




Simple random sampling
A method in which every member of the population has an equal probability of selection.


Systematic sampling
Selection of every kth member from a list, after a random starting point.


Stratified sampling
Division of the population into subgroups, followed by random sampling within each subgroup.


Cluster sampling
Division of the population into groups, followed by random selection of entire groups.


Convenience sampling
Selection of the most accessible members of the population; not probability-based.


Purposive sampling
Selection based on the researcher's judgement about which cases are most informative.


Sampling frame
The list or device from which sample members are actually drawn.


Undercoverage
Systematic exclusion of certain population members from the sampling frame.

title: 4  Chapter 04: Sampling Methods
text:
4.10 Exercises

A supermarket wants to survey 200 customers about their shopping experience. Describe how you would implement each of the following: (a) simple random sampling, (b) systematic sampling, (c) stratified sampling by time of day. What are the main practical advantages of each?
A researcher surveys university students by standing outside the library and asking whoever walks by. What type of sampling is this? What are the main risks for any conclusions drawn from this sample?
A manufacturer wants to test the quality of output from five production lines. She randomly selects two of the five lines and tests every item produced on those lines over a one-day period. What type of sampling is this? What bias might result?
An online retailer has customer records for 3.2 million people. They want a sample of 5,000. Describe the steps for implementing simple random sampling in practice.
A consulting firm proposes quota sampling to study attitudes toward a new policy among three industry groups. The quotas match the industry groups’ sizes in the population. Explain why this is not equivalent to proportionate stratified sampling, and what additional step would make it so.

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, students should be able to:
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
Distinguish between quantitative and categorical variables.
Identify the four levels of measurement: nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio.
Explain the difference between independent and dependent variables.
Describe the distinction between discrete and continuous variables.
Explain why the type of variable determines the appropriate statistical methods.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.2 Introduction
Every statistical analysis begins with data, and data consists of variables. A variable is any characteristic that can take different values across observations. Height, job title, monthly revenue, product rating, number of support tickets, region, all of these are variables.
Not all variables are alike. Whether a variable is numerical or categorical, whether it has a meaningful zero point, whether its values can be ordered, these properties determine which statistical methods apply. Using the wrong method for the variable type is one of the most common analytical errors in practice, and it produces results that are mathematically valid but substantively meaningless.
This chapter establishes the vocabulary and classifications that all subsequent chapters assume.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.3 Quantitative and categorical variables
The first and most important distinction is between quantitative variables and categorical variables.

5.3.1 Quantitative variables
A quantitative variable takes numerical values that represent amounts or counts. Arithmetic operations, addition, subtraction, averaging, make sense on quantitative variables. The mean of a set of transaction values is a meaningful number. So is the difference between two delivery times, or the sum of units sold across regions.
Examples from management: - Revenue (in £) - Number of employees - Customer satisfaction score (if treated as numeric) - Delivery time in hours - Return on investment (%)


5.3.2 Categorical variables
A categorical variable places observations into groups or categories. The numbers used to code categories are labels, not quantities: if you code “Male” as 1 and “Female” as 2, the number 2 does not mean twice as much as 1. Arithmetic operations on category codes are meaningless.
Examples from management: - Department (Operations, Sales, Finance) - Product type - Region - Payment method - Whether a customer churned (Yes/No)
A special case worth noting: binary variables (also called dichotomous variables) have exactly two categories. Churned/not churned, passed/failed, promoted/not promoted. These often receive special treatment because they can be coded as 0 and 1, which enables specific methods such as logistic regression.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.4 Levels of measurement
Within the two broad categories, variables are further classified by level of measurement, a framework developed by psychologist Stanley Stevens in 1946. The four levels are nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio.

5.4.1 Nominal
Nominal variables name or label categories with no inherent order. Department names, product codes, countries, payment methods, none of these have a natural ranking. You can count how many observations fall into each category (frequency) and you can find the most common category (mode), but you cannot meaningfully order, subtract, or average nominal values.


5.4.2 Ordinal
Ordinal variables have categories with a meaningful order, but the gaps between categories are not necessarily equal. A five-point satisfaction rating from “Very dissatisfied” to “Very satisfied” is ordinal: you know that 4 is better than 3, but you cannot assume that the difference between 4 and 3 is the same size as the difference between 3 and 2.
This is the point most frequently violated in practice. Likert scale responses are ordinal. Reporting their mean and applying inferential tests designed for interval data is extremely common, and the debate about whether it is acceptable continues among statisticians. For this book: when in doubt, treat ordinal data as ordinal and use the appropriate methods (covered in Chapter 26).


5.4.3 Interval
Interval variables have a meaningful order and equal gaps between values, but no true zero point. Temperature in Celsius is the classic example: the difference between 10°C and 20°C is the same as the difference between 20°C and 30°C, but 0°C does not mean “no temperature.” You can meaningfully add and subtract, but ratios are not meaningful: 20°C is not twice as hot as 10°C.
Interval variables are less common in business data than they might appear. Calendar years and dates have interval properties (the gap between 2020 and 2022 equals the gap between 2022 and 2024) but no true zero.


5.4.4 Ratio
Ratio variables have a meaningful order, equal intervals, and a true zero, meaning that zero represents a genuine absence of the quantity. Revenue, weight, age, distance, and time elapsed all have this property. When revenue is zero, there is no revenue. Ratios are meaningful: a company with £10 million in revenue has twice the revenue of one with £5 million.
Most quantitative business variables are ratio variables. This is the highest level of measurement and supports the full range of arithmetic operations.

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.5 Discrete and continuous variables
Quantitative variables are further divided into discrete and continuous.
A discrete variable takes a countable number of values, usually whole numbers. The number of complaints received, the number of products on a shelf, the number of employees absent on a given day, these can only be integers. You cannot have 3.7 complaints.
A continuous variable can theoretically take any value within a range, including fractions. Time, weight, temperature, and distance are continuous: a delivery can take 2.37 hours, a product can weigh 0.842 kg.
In practice, the distinction matters for probability calculations. The probability that a continuous variable takes exactly one specific value is zero, because there are infinitely many possible values. Probabilities for continuous variables are always defined over ranges, not points.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.6 Independent and dependent variables
When a study examines a relationship between variables, it typically distinguishes between the independent variable and the dependent variable.
The dependent variable (DV) is the outcome being measured, what you are trying to explain or predict. In most management research, the DV is the thing the organisation cares about: sales, customer satisfaction, employee retention, defect rate.
The independent variable (IV) is the variable expected to influence or explain the dependent variable, what you think might cause or predict changes in the DV. In an experiment, the independent variable is what is deliberately manipulated. In observational research, it is what is being tested as a potential predictor.
Some examples:








Study question
Independent variable
Dependent variable




Does training increase sales performance?
Whether the employee received training
Sales performance score


Does store layout affect transaction value?
Layout type (old/new)
Average transaction value (£)


Does delivery speed predict repeat purchases?
Delivery time (hours)
Whether customer made a repeat purchase



In regression analysis, the independent variable is often called the predictor or explanatory variable, and the dependent variable is called the response or outcome variable.
The labels “independent” and “dependent” are not always an accurate description of causation, in observational data, you cannot always be sure which direction causation runs. But the labels identify the analytical role each variable plays: the IV is what you put on the right side of the equation; the DV is what you are trying to account for.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
section: 5.7 Why variable type matters for method selection
text:
5.7 Why variable type matters for method selection
The level of measurement and variable type determine which statistical methods are appropriate. A brief preview:

Nominal DV: frequency analysis, chi-square tests, logistic regression.
Ordinal DV: median, percentiles, non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney, Kruskal-Wallis).
Interval or ratio DV: mean, standard deviation, t-tests, ANOVA, linear regression.

Computing the mean of an ordinal variable may not be mathematically impossible, but it is analytically dubious. Applying a t-test to a nominal outcome is simply incorrect. The method must match the data.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.8 Worked example


Code
# A dataset from a hotel chain, illustrating variable types in a real context
set.seed(303)

hotel_data <- data.frame(
  guest_id      = 1:200,
  # Nominal
  room_type     = sample(c("Standard", "Deluxe", "Suite"), 200, replace = TRUE,
                          prob = c(0.55, 0.30, 0.15)),
  payment       = sample(c("Card", "Cash", "Points"), 200, replace = TRUE,
                          prob = c(0.70, 0.10, 0.20)),
  # Ordinal
  satisfaction  = sample(1:5, 200, replace = TRUE,
                          prob = c(0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.35, 0.30)),
  # Ratio
  spend_gbp     = round(rnorm(200, mean = 180, sd = 55), 2),
  nights        = sample(1:7, 200, replace = TRUE,
                          prob = c(0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.10, 0.07, 0.05, 0.03)),
  # Binary (special case of nominal)
  returned      = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), 200, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.45, 0.55))
)

# Appropriate summaries by variable type
cat("--- Nominal: room_type ---\n")


--- Nominal: room_type ---


Code
print(table(hotel_data$room_type))



  Deluxe Standard    Suite 
      76       99       25 


Code
cat("\n--- Ordinal: satisfaction ---\n")



--- Ordinal: satisfaction ---


Code
print(table(hotel_data$satisfaction))



 1  2  3  4  5 
13 19 41 75 52 


Code
cat("Median satisfaction:", median(hotel_data$satisfaction), "\n")


Median satisfaction: 4 


Code
cat("\n--- Ratio: spend_gbp ---\n")



--- Ratio: spend_gbp ---


Code
cat("Mean spend:  £", round(mean(hotel_data$spend_gbp), 2), "\n")


Mean spend:  £ 185.54 


Code
cat("Median spend: £", round(median(hotel_data$spend_gbp), 2), "\n")


Median spend: £ 184.32 


Code
cat("SD:          £", round(sd(hotel_data$spend_gbp), 2), "\n")


SD:          £ 51.17 


Notice that the appropriate summary statistic differs by variable type. For the nominal room type, we count. For the ordinal satisfaction rating, the median is more defensible than the mean. For the ratio spend variable, the mean and standard deviation are fully appropriate.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.9 Summary
A variable is any characteristic that varies across observations. Variables are classified as quantitative (numerical amounts) or categorical (group labels). Quantitative variables divide further into discrete (countable values) and continuous (any value within a range).
The level of measurement, nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio, determines what operations make sense. Nominal variables name categories with no order. Ordinal variables have order but unequal gaps. Interval variables have equal gaps but no true zero. Ratio variables have all of the above plus a meaningful zero.
In any study examining relationships, the dependent variable is the outcome being explained, and the independent variable is the potential predictor or cause. Variable type governs which statistical methods are appropriate: using the wrong method for the variable type produces results that are technically computed but substantively wrong.
crumbs: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales

title: 5  Chapter 05: Types of Variables, Independent and Dependent Variables, and Measurement Scales
text:
5.10 Key terms



Table 05.2: Key terms for Chapter 05


Term
Definition




Variable
Any characteristic that takes different values across cases in a dataset.


Categorical variable
A variable whose values are group labels or names rather than quantities.


Numerical variable
A variable whose values are numbers representing measurable quantities.


Nominal scale
A measurement scale in which categories are named but have no inherent order.


Ordinal scale
A scale in which categories have a meaningful order but the intervals are not equal.


Interval scale
A scale with equal intervals between values but no true zero point.


Ratio scale
A scale with equal intervals and a true zero point; ratios between values are meaningful.


Discrete variable
A numerical variable that takes only distinct, countable values.


Continuous variable
A numerical variable that can take any value within a range.

5.11 Exercises

Classify each of the following as quantitative or categorical, and state the level of measurement: (a) customer postcode, (b) number of items in a shopping basket, (c) annual salary (£), (d) employee satisfaction rated 1–10, (e) temperature in a warehouse (°C), (f) whether a payment was made on time.

Why is it problematic to compute the mean of a Likert scale response (e.g., “On a scale of 1–5, how satisfied are you?”)? Under what conditions might it be considered acceptable?

Give an example from your own field of a ratio variable and an ordinal variable. For each, explain one statistical operation that is appropriate and one that is not.

title: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency
text:
6.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency

title: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency
text:
Construct a frequency distribution for categorical and numerical data.
Calculate relative frequencies and cumulative frequencies.
Choose appropriate class intervals for a numerical frequency distribution.
Interpret a histogram and a cumulative frequency curve.
Use frequency distributions to answer practical questions about a dataset.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency

title: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency
text:
6.2 Introduction
Raw data is rarely informative on its own. A spreadsheet containing 120 insurance claim amounts tells a manager almost nothing until those numbers are organised. Frequency distributions provide that organisation. They group observations into categories or intervals and count how many fall into each group, converting an unreadable list into a structured summary.
This chapter introduces frequency distributions for both categorical and numerical data, extends them to relative and cumulative forms, and shows how histograms make the same information visual. The dataset for this unit contains records of 120 insurance claims, including claim amount, claim type, region, processing time, and settlement status.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency

title: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency
text:
6.3 What a frequency distribution is
A frequency distribution is a table that shows how many observations fall into each category or interval. The count in each class is the absolute frequency. Two derived measures add proportional context.
The relative frequency of a class is its absolute frequency divided by the total number of observations. It expresses each class as a proportion of the whole, and the relative frequencies across all classes sum to one.
The cumulative frequency of a class is the total number of observations in that class and all classes below it. Cumulative frequency answers questions of the form: how many observations lie below a given value?
crumbs: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency

title: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency
text:
6.4 Frequency distributions for categorical data
When the variable of interest is categorical, a frequency distribution simply counts the number of observations in each named group.
The insurance dataset contains a variable recording the type of each claim. The table below shows the frequency distribution for claim type.


Code
library(dplyr)

cat_freq <- df |>
  count(policy_type, name = "Frequency") |>
  mutate(
    Relative_frequency = round(Frequency / sum(Frequency), 3),
    Percentage         = round(100 * Frequency / sum(Frequency), 1)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(Frequency))

knitr::kable(
  cat_freq,
  col.names = c("Policy type", "Frequency", "Relative frequency", "Percentage"),
  caption   = "Table 6.1: Frequency distribution of insurance claims by policy type"
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width        = FALSE
  )



Table 6.1: Frequency distribution of insurance claims by policy type

The relative frequency column shows what proportion of all claims each type accounts for. These proportions sum to one. The percentage column rescales them to a more familiar form.
crumbs: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency

title: 6  Chapter 06: Frequency Distributions and Cumulative Frequency
text:
6.5 Frequency distributions for numerical data
When the variable is numerical, observations must first be grouped into class intervals before counting. The choice of interval boundaries affects how the distribution looks and what it reveals.

6.5.1 Choosing class intervals
A practical rule is to use between 5 and 15 classes for most business datasets. Fewer than 5 classes obscures detail; more than 15 fragments the data into noise. A convenient starting formula is:
\[\text{Class width} \approx \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Number of classes}}\]
Round the result to a convenient figure and keep all intervals equal in width. Open-ended classes at either extreme should be avoided where possible.


6.5.2 Building the table
The insurance dataset includes claim amounts ranging from small travel claims to large health and property claims. The table below groups these into intervals of GBP 2,000.


Code
breaks <- c(0, 2000, 4000, 6000, 8000, 10000, 12000, 14000, 16000)
labels <- c("0-2,000", "2,001-4,000", "4,001-6,000", "6,001-8,000",
            "8,001-10,000", "10,001-12,000", "12,001-14,000", "14,001-16,000")

num_freq <- df |>
  mutate(class = cut(claim_amount_gbp, breaks = breaks,
                     labels = labels, include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  count(class, name = "Frequency", .drop = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    Relative  = round(Frequency / sum(Frequency), 3),
    Cumulative = cumsum(Frequency),
    Cum_rel    = round(cumsum(Relative), 3)
  )

knitr::kable(
  num_freq,
  col.names = c("Claim amount (GBP)", "Frequency", "Relative frequency",
                "Cumulative frequency", "Cumulative relative frequency"),
  caption   = "Table 6.2: Frequency distribution of claim amounts"
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width        = FALSE
  )
