A quality inspector checks 8 items. The defect rate for this product line is 6%. What is the probability that exactly 2 items are defective? That at least 1 is defective?

text:
15.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
Construct and interpret a scatter plot for two quantitative variables.
Describe the direction, form, and strength of a relationship from a scatter plot.
Calculate and interpret Pearson’s correlation coefficient.
Explain the limitations of correlation, including sensitivity to outliers.
Distinguish correlation from causation.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.2 Introduction
Many of the most important questions in management concern relationships between variables. Does higher advertising spending produce higher sales? Do employees who receive more training achieve higher productivity? Does customer satisfaction predict repeat purchases?
Scatter plots and correlation provide the first tools for investigating these questions. They reveal whether a relationship exists, what direction it takes, and how consistently the two variables move together. This chapter works with performance data from 120 retail stores, with variables covering advertising spend, footfall, weekly sales, promotional activity, and competitive environment.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.3 Scatter plots
A scatter plot displays the joint distribution of two quantitative variables. Each observation is plotted as a point, with one variable on the horizontal axis and the other on the vertical axis. The resulting cloud of points reveals the pattern of the relationship.


Code
library(dplyr)
ggplot(df, aes(x = ad_spend_gbp, y = weekly_sales_gbp)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#1B3A5C", alpha = 0.65, size = 2.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "#C0392B",
              linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Weekly advertising spend (GBP)",
       y = "Weekly sales (GBP)",
       title = "Advertising spend and weekly sales across 120 stores") +
  theme_dmm()





Figure 15.1: Advertising spend and weekly sales




The shaded band around the trend line is the 95% confidence region for the line’s position. Points more spread around the line indicate more noise in the relationship.

15.3.1 What to look for in a scatter plot
Three features should be assessed when reading a scatter plot.
Direction. A positive relationship means that as one variable increases, the other tends to increase. A negative relationship means that as one increases, the other tends to decrease.
Form. Most business relationships are approximately linear — the points cluster around a straight line. Non-linear forms are possible but require different analytical tools.
Strength. A strong relationship means the points cluster tightly around the trend. A weak relationship means they are widely scattered with only a vague pattern.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.4 Pearson’s correlation coefficient
The scatter plot describes a relationship visually. Pearson’s correlation coefficient, denoted r, quantifies its strength and direction in a single number.
\[r = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}\]
The coefficient r always lies between -1 and 1. A value of 1 indicates a perfect positive linear relationship; -1 a perfect negative one; 0 no linear relationship.


Code
r_ads_sales    <- cor(df$ad_spend_gbp, df$weekly_sales_gbp)
r_foot_sales   <- cor(df$weekly_footfall, df$weekly_sales_gbp)
r_ads_foot     <- cor(df$ad_spend_gbp, df$weekly_footfall)

cor_table <- data.frame(
  Pair = c("Ad spend vs Weekly sales",
           "Footfall vs Weekly sales",
           "Ad spend vs Footfall"),
  r    = round(c(r_ads_sales, r_foot_sales, r_ads_foot), 3)
)

knitr::kable(cor_table,
  col.names = c("Variable pair", "Pearson r"),
  caption   = "Table 15.1: Correlation coefficients for key variable pairs") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 15.1: Correlation coefficients for key variable pairs


Variable pair
Pearson r




Ad spend vs Weekly sales
0.661


Footfall vs Weekly sales
0.831


Ad spend vs Footfall
0.642








Table 15.2: Conventional interpretation of correlation strength


Range of |r|
Conventional label




|r| >= 0.90
Very strong


0.70 <= |r| < 0.90
Strong


0.50 <= |r| < 0.70
Moderate


0.30 <= |r| < 0.50
Weak


|r| < 0.30
Very weak or none





These labels are conventions, not thresholds. What counts as a strong correlation depends on the field and the question being asked. In social sciences, r = 0.40 may be noteworthy. In precision engineering, r = 0.95 might be considered modest.

  group_by(store_format) |>
  summarise(

crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.7 Limitations of correlation

15.7.1 Sensitivity to outliers
A single extreme observation can substantially change the value of r.


Code
# Correlation with and without the top-spending store
r_full    <- cor(df$ad_spend_gbp, df$weekly_sales_gbp)
df_trim   <- df |> filter(ad_spend_gbp < quantile(ad_spend_gbp, 0.98))
r_trimmed <- cor(df_trim$ad_spend_gbp, df_trim$weekly_sales_gbp)

outlier_table <- data.frame(
  Dataset = c("All 120 stores", "Top 2% ad spenders removed"),
  r       = round(c(r_full, r_trimmed), 3),
  n       = c(nrow(df), nrow(df_trim))
)
knitr::kable(outlier_table,
  col.names = c("Dataset", "r", "n"),
  caption   = "Table 15.5: Effect of outliers on correlation") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 15.5: Effect of outliers on correlation


Dataset
r
n




All 120 stores
0.661
120


Top 2% ad spenders removed
0.641
117







15.7.2 Correlation measures only linear relationships
Pearson’s r measures the strength of the linear component of a relationship. If two variables have a strong curved (non-linear) relationship, r may be near zero even though the variables are clearly related. Always inspect the scatter plot before relying on r.





Figure 15.2: Promotion status and the ad-sales relationship




Colouring by a third variable reveals that promotion-active stores have a different intercept in the scatter plot, meaning they achieve higher sales at the same level of advertising spend. A single correlation coefficient would fail to capture this nuance.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.8 Worked example






Note



Scenario. A regional director wants to know whether stores with more months of operation are associated with higher weekly sales. Calculate the relevant correlation coefficient, plot the relationship, and interpret the result.




Code
r_months_sales <- cor(df$months_open, df$weekly_sales_gbp)
cat("r (months open vs weekly sales) =", round(r_months_sales, 3), "\n")


r (months open vs weekly sales) = 0.232 


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = months_open, y = weekly_sales_gbp)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#1B3A5C", alpha = 0.65, size = 2.0) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              colour = "#C0392B", linewidth = 0.9) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Months open", y = "Weekly sales (GBP)",
       title = "Store maturity and weekly sales") +
  theme_dmm()





Figure 15.3: Months open and weekly sales




The correlation coefficient and scatter plot together show both the direction and strength of the relationship. A positive but modest r suggests that time open is one factor among several influencing sales performance. The scatter plot reveals whether the relationship is approximately linear and whether any stores stand out as unusual.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.9 Summary
A scatter plot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables. Key features to describe are direction (positive or negative), form (linear or non-linear), and strength (tight or loose clustering). Pearson’s correlation coefficient r quantifies linear strength and direction on a scale from -1 to 1.
Correlation has important limitations. It is sensitive to outliers, measures only linear relationships, can differ across subgroups, and does not establish causation. A scatter plot should always accompany or precede any correlation calculation to avoid misinterpretation.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.10 Key terms



Table 15.6: Key terms for Chapter 15


Term
Definition




Scatter plot
A chart displaying each observation as a point on axes representing two quantitative variables.


Positive correlation
A relationship in which higher values of one variable tend to coincide with higher values of the other.


Negative correlation
A relationship in which higher values of one variable tend to coincide with lower values of the other.


Pearson's r
A coefficient between -1 and 1 measuring the strength and direction of a linear relationship.


Correlation matrix
A table showing Pearson's r for all pairs of numeric variables in a dataset.


Spurious correlation
A correlation that appears in the data but does not reflect a genuine relationship between the two variables.
crumbs: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation

title: 15  Chapter 15: Scatter Plots and Correlation
text:
15.11 Exercises

Using Table 15.1, which variable pair has the strongest correlation with weekly sales? What does this suggest about where management should focus attention?
A scatter plot of advertising spend against weekly sales shows a positive trend but several points are far from the line. A colleague reports r = 0.63 and says “advertising spend explains 63% of the variation in sales.” What is wrong with this interpretation?
Using Table 15.4, the correlation between ad spend and sales varies by store format. What does this imply for how the company should analyse and act on this relationship?
Identify one pair of variables in everyday life that would likely have a high positive correlation but where the correlation is clearly not causal. Explain why the relationship exists without one variable causing the other.
A dataset shows r = 0.05 between two variables. A manager concludes: “There is no relationship between these variables.” Under what circumstances would this conclusion be wrong?

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
Fit a simple linear regression model and interpret the intercept and slope.
Use the regression equation to make predictions within the range of the data.
Interpret \(R^2\) as a measure of model fit.
Explain why correlation does not imply causation.
Identify confounding variables and explain their role in observed associations.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.2 Introduction
Correlation describes the strength and direction of a linear relationship. Regression goes further: it fits a specific equation to the data, allowing predictions and a more precise description of how one variable changes with another.
This chapter introduces simple linear regression, which models the relationship between one predictor variable and one response variable. It then addresses one of the most important ideas in applied statistics: the difference between finding an association and establishing a cause.
The dataset for this unit contains records for 200 employees, with variables covering training hours, productivity scores, salary, tenure, and promotion status.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.3 Simple linear regression
Simple linear regression expresses the relationship between a response variable Y and a predictor variable X as a straight line:
\[\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x\]
where \(b_0\) is the intercept (the predicted value of Y when X equals zero) and \(b_1\) is the slope (the change in predicted Y for a one-unit increase in X). The hat on \(\hat{y}\) indicates that this is a predicted, not an observed, value.
The coefficients \(b_0\) and \(b_1\) are chosen to minimise the sum of squared differences between the observed values and the predicted values. This method is called ordinary least squares (OLS).
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
section: 16.4 Fitting and interpreting a regression model
text:
16.4 Fitting and interpreting a regression model


Code
model <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours, data = df)
b0 <- round(coef(model)[1], 3)
b1 <- round(coef(model)[2], 3)
r2 <- round(summary(model)$r.squared, 3)

cat("Intercept (b0):", b0, "\n")


Intercept (b0): 45.733 


Code
cat("Slope     (b1):", b1, "\n")


Slope     (b1): 0.661 


Code
cat("R-squared     :", r2, "\n")


R-squared     : 0.345 





Table 16.1: Regression results — productivity on training hours



Term
Value
Interpretation




(Intercept)
Intercept (b0)

16.4.1 The regression line on the scatter plot

crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.5 Making predictions
The regression equation can be used to predict values of Y for given values of X.


Code
# Predicted productivity for 20, 30, and 40 training hours
new_data <- data.frame(training_hours = c(20, 30, 40))
preds    <- predict(model, newdata = new_data)

pred_table <- data.frame(
  training_hours    = new_data$training_hours,
  predicted_score   = round(preds, 1)
)

knitr::kable(pred_table,
  col.names = c("Training hours", "Predicted productivity score"),
  caption   = "Table 16.2: Predicted productivity for selected training levels") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 16.2: Predicted productivity for selected training levels


Training hours
Predicted productivity score




20
59.0


30
65.6


40
72.2





Predictions are most reliable within the range of the observed training hours. Extrapolation beyond this range produces predictions the model was not designed to make and should be treated with caution.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.6 R-squared
\(R^2\), the coefficient of determination, measures what proportion of the variation in Y is accounted for by the linear relationship with X.
\[R^2 = r^2\]
where r is Pearson’s correlation coefficient. \(R^2\) ranges from 0 to 1. A value of 0.42, for instance, means that 42% of the variability in productivity scores is accounted for by variation in training hours. The remaining 58% is due to other factors not in the model.
\(R^2\) does not tell you whether the model is appropriate or whether the relationship is causal. A high \(R^2\) simply means the line fits the data well — not that X causes Y.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.7 Residuals
A residual is the difference between an observed value and the value predicted by the model:
\[e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\]
Residuals should be examined to check whether the linear model is appropriate.





Figure 16.2: Residuals against fitted values




A good residual plot shows points scattered randomly around zero with no obvious pattern. Systematic patterns, such as a funnel shape or a curve, indicate that the linear model may not be appropriate or that the variance is not constant.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.8 Correlation versus causation
One of the most important principles in applied statistics is that correlation does not imply causation. Two variables can be strongly correlated for reasons that have nothing to do with one causing the other.

16.8.1 Three reasons for observed correlations
Direct causation. X causes Y. More training hours genuinely improve productivity. This is possible, but must be established by design, not just by observing a correlation.
Reverse causation. Y causes X. More productive employees may be selected for more training by their managers. If so, the causal direction is the reverse of what the regression implies.
Confounding. A third variable Z causes both X and Y. Employee tenure may drive both higher training allocations and higher productivity. The observed correlation between training and productivity may reflect the effect of tenure rather than a direct effect of training itself.


Code
# Does controlling for tenure change the training-productivity relationship?
model_full     <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours + tenure_years,
                     data = df)
b1_training_alone   <- round(coef(model)[2], 3)
b1_training_adj     <- round(coef(model_full)["training_hours"], 3)
b1_tenure           <- round(coef(model_full)["tenure_years"], 3)

confound_table <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Training only", "Training + Tenure"),
  b1_training = c(b1_training_alone, b1_training_adj),
  b1_tenure   = c(NA, b1_tenure),
  R2 = round(c(summary(model)$r.squared,
               summary(model_full)$r.squared), 3)
)

knitr::kable(confound_table,
  col.names = c("Model", "Slope (training)", "Slope (tenure)", "R²"),
  caption   = "Table 16.3: Confounding: training effect with and without tenure") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 16.3: Confounding: training effect with and without tenure



Model
Slope (training)
Slope (tenure)
R²





Training only
0.661
NA
0.345


tenure_years
Training + Tenure
0.645
0.939
0.475





If the slope for training hours changes substantially when tenure is added to the model, this is evidence that tenure is a confounding variable. The apparent training effect may partly, or entirely, reflect the effect of tenure on productivity.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.9 Establishing causation
Correlation is necessary but not sufficient for causation. Establishing causation requires:

Temporal order: X must precede Y in time.
Covariation: X and Y must be correlated.
Elimination of alternatives: alternative explanations, including confounders, must be ruled out.

The strongest evidence for causation comes from randomised controlled experiments, where participants are randomly assigned to receive or not receive the treatment of interest. When random assignment is not possible, observational data require careful analysis and strong subject-matter knowledge to support causal claims.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.10 Worked example






Note



Scenario. A training manager presents a regression showing that each additional training hour is associated with a higher productivity score. She concludes that the company should increase training hours for all employees. Evaluate this conclusion using the tools introduced in this chapter.




Code
# Step 1: fit the simple model
m1 <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours, data = df)

# Step 2: add potential confounders
m2 <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours + tenure_years, data = df)
m3 <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours + tenure_years +
            salary_gbp, data = df)

model_compare <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Training only", "+ Tenure", "+ Tenure + Salary"),
  b_training = round(c(coef(m1)[2], coef(m2)[2], coef(m3)[2]), 3),
  R2 = round(c(summary(m1)$r.squared,
               summary(m2)$r.squared,
               summary(m3)$r.squared), 3)
)

knitr::kable(model_compare,
  col.names = c("Model", "Training coefficient", "R²"),
  caption   = "Table 16.4: Sensitivity of training effect to model specification") |>

If the training coefficient remains stable across models, there is more confidence that the relationship is genuine. If it changes substantially, the simple model is likely confounded. Even a stable coefficient does not establish causation: an experiment randomising training allocation would be needed for that.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.11 Summary
Simple linear regression fits a straight line to the relationship between a predictor and a response variable. The slope gives the change in predicted Y for a one-unit increase in X. The intercept gives the predicted Y when X is zero. \(R^2\) measures the proportion of variation in Y explained by the model.
Residuals should be examined to assess whether the linear model is appropriate. Correlation does not imply causation: an observed association may reflect direct causation, reverse causation, or confounding. Establishing causation requires temporal order, covariation, and the elimination of alternative explanations, most reliably through a randomised experiment.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.12 Key terms



Table 16.5: Key terms for Chapter 16


Term
Definition




Simple linear regression
A model expressing the linear relationship between one predictor and one response variable.


Intercept (b0)
The predicted value of Y when X equals zero.


Slope (b1)
The predicted change in Y for a one-unit increase in X.


Ordinary least squares
The method of estimating regression coefficients by minimising the sum of squared residuals.


R-squared
The proportion of variation in Y accounted for by the linear relationship with X; ranges from 0 to 1.


Residual
The difference between an observed value and the value predicted by the model.


Extrapolation
Using a regression model to predict Y for X values outside the range of the observed data.


Confounding variable
A variable that is associated with both X and Y, which can create or distort an apparent relationship between them.
crumbs: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation

title: 16  Chapter 16: Regression and Correlation versus Causation
text:
16.13 Exercises

Using the fitted model in Table 16.1, predict the productivity score for an employee who completed 35 training hours. Is this prediction within or outside the range of the observed data?
\(R^2\) for the training-productivity model is 0.345. Interpret this value. What does the remaining unexplained variation represent?
A study finds that cities with more ice cream sales have higher crime rates. A journalist concludes that ice cream causes crime. Identify the confounding variable and explain how it produces the observed correlation.
Using Table 16.3, how does the training coefficient change when tenure is added to the model? What does this tell you about the nature of the training-productivity relationship?
A manager says: “Our regression shows that each year of tenure increases predicted productivity by a fixed amount. We should therefore prioritise keeping experienced staff over recruiting new ones.” What assumptions is she making, and what evidence would you want before accepting this recommendation?

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Explain what a sampling distribution is and why it matters for inference.
State the central limit theorem and its conditions.
Calculate and interpret the standard error of the mean.
Explain the relationship between sample size and the precision of an estimate.
Describe why the central limit theorem makes normal-based inference broadly applicable.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.2 Introduction
The gap between description and inference was first identified in Chapter 02. Description summarises the data at hand. Inference draws conclusions about a larger population from a sample. The bridge between them is the sampling distribution: the distribution of a statistic, such as the sample mean, across all possible samples of a given size from a population.
Understanding the sampling distribution is essential because it tells us how much a sample statistic is likely to deviate from the corresponding population parameter simply by chance. This uncertainty, quantified as the standard error, is the basis of confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and almost every inferential procedure in the remaining chapters.
The dataset for this unit is a population of 2,000 warehouse orders, with variables including fulfilment hours, weight, category, and distance. We treat it as the full population and repeatedly draw samples to illustrate what sampling distributions look like in practice.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.3 What is a sampling distribution?
Suppose the true population mean fulfilment time for all 2,000 orders is \(\mu\). We do not know this in real work, but here we can compute it directly.


Code
library(dplyr)

pop_mean <- mean(df$fulfilment_hours)
pop_sd   <- sd(df$fulfilment_hours)
pop_n    <- nrow(df)

params <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("Population mean (mu)", "Population SD (sigma)",
                "Population size (N)"),
  Value     = round(c(pop_mean, pop_sd, pop_n), 2)
)

knitr::kable(params,
  col.names = c("Parameter", "Value"),
  caption   = "Table 17.1: True population parameters for fulfilment hours") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 17.1: True population parameters for fulfilment hours


Parameter
Value




Population mean (mu)
12.16


Population SD (sigma)
3.39


Population size (N)
2000.00





Now suppose a researcher observes only a sample of 40 orders and computes the sample mean. Their value will differ from \(\mu\) — that difference is sampling error. If a second researcher independently takes a different sample of 40, they will obtain a slightly different mean. The sampling distribution of the mean is the distribution of all such sample means across all possible samples of size n.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.4 Demonstrating the sampling distribution


Code
set.seed(42)
sample_means_n30  <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(df$fulfilment_hours, 30)))
sample_means_n100 <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(df$fulfilment_hours, 100)))

samp_summary <- data.frame(
  Sample_size = c(30, 100),
  Mean_of_means = round(c(mean(sample_means_n30),
                           mean(sample_means_n100)), 3),
  SD_of_means   = round(c(sd(sample_means_n30),
                           sd(sample_means_n100)), 3),
  Theoretical_SE = round(c(pop_sd / sqrt(30),
                            pop_sd / sqrt(100)), 3)
)

knitr::kable(samp_summary,
  col.names = c("n", "Mean of sample means",
                "SD of sample means (observed SE)",
                "Theoretical SE (sigma/sqrt(n))"),
  caption   = "Table 17.2: Sampling distributions for two sample sizes") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 17.2: Sampling distributions for two sample sizes


n
Mean of sample means
SD of sample means (observed SE)
Theoretical SE (sigma/sqrt(n))




30
12.137
0.629

100
12.136

Two features are visible in Table 17.2. First, the mean of all sample means is very close to the population mean: sample means cluster around the true value. Second, as sample size increases, the spread of the sample means decreases. The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is the standard error.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.5 The standard error
The standard error of the mean is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. For a population with standard deviation \(\sigma\) and sample size \(n\):
\[SE_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
In practice, \(\sigma\) is usually unknown and is estimated by the sample standard deviation \(s\):
\[\widehat{SE}_{\bar{x}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]
The standard error decreases as sample size increases. To halve the standard error, the sample size must be quadrupled.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.6 The central limit theorem
The central limit theorem (CLT) states that, regardless of the shape of the population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean approaches a normal distribution as the sample size increases.
More precisely: if random samples of size \(n\) are drawn from a population with mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\), then for sufficiently large \(n\):
\[\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)\]
The practical implication is that normal-based inference is valid even when the population is not normally distributed, provided the sample is large enough.





Figure 17.1: The CLT in action — population vs sampling distributions




The population distribution is right-skewed. The sampling distributions for n = 30 and n = 100 are both approximately bell-shaped and centred on the population mean. The n = 100 distribution is narrower, reflecting greater precision.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.7 How large is “sufficiently large”?
The CLT applies faster (with smaller n) when the population is already approximately symmetric. For moderately skewed populations, n ≥ 30 is a commonly used rule of thumb. For heavily skewed distributions or those with extreme outliers, larger samples are needed.





Figure 17.2: Sampling distributions for four sample sizes
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.8 The square root law
\[SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
This relationship has a practical implication for survey design and quality control: precision improves with sample size, but at a diminishing rate.



Table 17.3: Standard error at different sample sizes


Sample size n
Standard error (estimated)




10
1.072


25
0.678


50
0.480


100
0.339


200
0.240


400
0.170


1000
0.107





Doubling from n = 50 to n = 100 reduces the SE by roughly 30%. Doubling from n = 400 to n = 800 produces the same proportional reduction, but costs four times as many additional observations. This diminishing return shapes the cost-benefit analysis of sample size decisions.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.9 Summary
A sampling distribution is the distribution of a sample statistic across all possible samples of a given size. The sampling distribution of the mean has a mean equal to the population mean and a standard deviation equal to the standard error, \(\sigma / \sqrt{n}\).
The central limit theorem guarantees that this sampling distribution approaches normality as n increases, regardless of the shape of the population. This is the theoretical foundation for all normal-based confidence intervals and hypothesis tests covered in the remaining chapters.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.10 Key terms



Table 17.4: Key terms for Chapter 17


Term
Definition




Sampling distribution
The distribution of a sample statistic across all possible samples of a given size from a population.


Standard error
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of a statistic; for the mean, SE = sigma / sqrt(n).


Central limit theorem
The theorem stating that the sampling distribution of the sample mean approaches normality as sample size increases, regardless of the population shape.


Square root law
The relationship SE = sigma / sqrt(n), implying that quadrupling the sample size halves the standard error.
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem
text:
17.11 Exercises

Using Table 17.2, the observed SE for n = 30 is close to but not exactly equal to the theoretical SE. Why might they differ slightly?
A population has mean 50 and standard deviation 12. What is the standard error of the mean for samples of size 36? Of size 144? How does the SE change when the sample size quadruples?
The population of fulfilment hours is right-skewed. Looking at Figure 17.2, at what sample size do the sampling distributions begin to look approximately normal? What does this tell you about applying the CLT to this population?
A manager wants to estimate the mean delivery time to within 2 minutes with a standard error of 1 minute. If the population standard deviation is 8 minutes, what sample size is required?
A colleague says: “The CLT says that if we take a large enough sample, the data will be normally distributed.” Is this correct? What does the CLT actually say?
crumbs: 17  Chapter 17: Sampling Distributions and the Central Limit Theorem

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
Explain what a confidence interval represents.
Construct a confidence interval for a population mean using the t-distribution.
Interpret a confidence interval correctly and avoid common misinterpretations.
Explain the effect of confidence level and sample size on interval width.
Construct and compare confidence intervals across subgroups.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.2 Introduction
Chapter 17 established that a sample mean is a point estimate of the population mean: it is a single best guess. But a single number conveys no information about how uncertain that guess is. A confidence interval replaces the point estimate with a range of plausible values, communicating both the estimate and the uncertainty surrounding it.
Confidence intervals are used constantly in applied work: in survey reports (“satisfaction is 3.8, margin of error ±0.2”), in clinical trials, in market research, and in operations analysis. The dataset for this unit contains 240 food delivery records, with variables including delivery time, distance, cuisine type, and driver rating.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.3 What a confidence interval is
A confidence interval is a range of values constructed from sample data such that, if the sampling procedure were repeated many times, a specified proportion of the intervals would contain the true population parameter.
A 95% confidence interval means: if we were to draw many independent samples and construct a confidence interval from each, approximately 95% of those intervals would contain the true population mean. It does not mean there is a 95% probability that the true mean lies in this specific interval — once the interval is computed, the true mean either is or is not inside it.
This distinction matters. The confidence refers to the long-run reliability of the procedure, not to the probability of a single outcome.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
section: 18.4 Constructing a confidence interval for the mean
text:
18.4 Constructing a confidence interval for the mean
When the population standard deviation is unknown (which is almost always the case in practice), the confidence interval for the population mean uses the t-distribution:
\[\bar{x} \pm t^* \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\]
where \(t^*\) is the critical value from the t-distribution with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom, and \(s/\sqrt{n}\) is the estimated standard error.


Code
library(dplyr)

n      <- nrow(df)
x_bar  <- mean(df$delivery_minutes)
s      <- sd(df$delivery_minutes)
se     <- s / sqrt(n)
t_star <- qt(0.975, df = n - 1)   # 95% CI, two-tailed

lower_95 <- x_bar - t_star * se
upper_95 <- x_bar + t_star * se

ci_table <- data.frame(
  Statistic = c("Sample size (n)", "Sample mean", "Sample SD",
                "Standard error", "t* (df = n-1)", "Lower bound (95%)",
                "Upper bound (95%)"),
  Value = round(c(n, x_bar, s, se, t_star, lower_95, upper_95), 3)
)

knitr::kable(ci_table,
  col.names = c("Statistic", "Value"),
  caption   = "Table 18.1: 95% confidence interval for mean delivery time") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"),
                             full_width = FALSE)

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.5 Using R’s t.test() function


Code

ttest_output <- data.frame(
  Item  = c("Sample mean", "Lower 95% CI", "Upper 95% CI"),
  Value = round(c(result$estimate,
                  result$conf.int[1],
                  result$conf.int[2]), 3)
)

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
section: 18.6 Effect of confidence level on interval width
text:
18.6 Effect of confidence level on interval width
A higher confidence level requires a wider interval to capture the true parameter more reliably.


Code
levels_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(0.80, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99), function(cl) {
  res <- t.test(df$delivery_minutes, conf.level = cl)
  data.frame(
    Confidence_level = paste0(cl * 100, "%"),
    Lower = round(res$conf.int[1], 2),
    Upper = round(res$conf.int[2], 2),
    Width = round(diff(res$conf.int), 2)
  )
}))

knitr::kable(levels_df,
  col.names = c("Confidence level", "Lower bound",
                "Upper bound", "Interval width (mins)"),
  caption   = "Table 18.3: Confidence intervals at different confidence levels") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)

Increasing confidence from 95% to 99% widens the interval. There is a trade-off: higher confidence comes at the cost of less precision.

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
section: 18.7 Effect of sample size on interval width
text:
18.7 Effect of sample size on interval width
For a fixed confidence level, a larger sample produces a narrower interval because the standard error decreases.





Figure 18.1: Interval width narrows as sample size increases

If the two confidence intervals do not overlap, there is good informal evidence that the population means differ. Overlapping intervals are less conclusive: they do not rule out a difference. A formal two-sample test, covered in Chapter 23, provides a definitive answer.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.9 Common misinterpretations



Table 18.5: Common misinterpretations of confidence intervals


Incorrect interpretation
Correct interpretation




There is a 95% probability that the true mean lies in this interval.
The interval was constructed using a procedure that captures the true mean in 95% of repeated applications. For this specific interval, the true mean either is or is not inside it.


95% of individual delivery times fall within the interval.
The interval estimates the population mean, not the spread of individual observations. Individual times vary far more widely.


A wider interval is always less useful than a narrower one.
A wider interval at a higher confidence level may be more honest about the true uncertainty than a narrower interval at a lower level.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.10 Summary
A confidence interval provides a range of plausible values for a population parameter, communicating both the estimate and its uncertainty. For a population mean with unknown \(\sigma\), the interval is \(\bar{x} \pm t^* \cdot (s/\sqrt{n})\). Confidence refers to the long-run reliability of the procedure, not to the probability that the true mean lies in any specific computed interval.
A higher confidence level produces a wider interval. A larger sample produces a narrower interval for a fixed confidence level. These trade-offs inform sample size planning in survey and experimental design.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.11 Key terms



Table 18.6: Key terms for Chapter 18


Term
Definition




Confidence interval
A range of values constructed from sample data using a procedure that captures the true parameter a specified percentage of the time.


Confidence level
The proportion of intervals that would contain the true parameter if the sampling procedure were repeated many times; typically 90%, 95%, or 99%.


t-distribution
A family of symmetric bell-shaped distributions indexed by degrees of freedom; used when sigma is unknown.


Critical value (t*)
The t-value such that the area between -t* and t* under the t-distribution equals the confidence level.


Margin of error
Half the width of the confidence interval: t* times the standard error.


Degrees of freedom
A parameter of the t-distribution equal to n-1 for a one-sample mean interval.
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means
text:
18.12 Exercises

A sample of 36 call durations has mean 4.8 minutes and standard deviation 1.6 minutes. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the population mean. Interpret the interval in plain language.
Using Table 18.3, what confidence level would you recommend for a food delivery company reporting average delivery times to customers? Justify your answer.
Using Table 18.4, the confidence intervals for weekday and weekend deliveries overlap. Does this mean weekend and weekday mean delivery times are the same? What would you do next?
A researcher doubles her sample size from 60 to 120. By approximately what factor does the width of her 95% confidence interval change?
A report states: “We are 95% confident that the true mean is in the interval [28.4, 31.6] minutes.” A colleague says: “That means 95% of deliveries take between 28.4 and 31.6 minutes.” How would you correct this?
crumbs: 18  Chapter 18: Confidence Intervals for Means

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
Construct a confidence interval for a population proportion.
Interpret a proportion confidence interval correctly.
Determine the sample size required for a desired margin of error.
Compare confidence intervals for proportions across subgroups.
Explain the role of the conservative estimate \(\hat{p} = 0.5\) in sample size planning.
crumbs: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.2 Introduction
Many business questions concern proportions rather than means. What fraction of customers are satisfied? What proportion of products pass quality inspection? What percentage of survey respondents would recommend the company to others?
Confidence intervals for proportions follow the same logic as those for means but use the normal approximation rather than the t-distribution. Sample size planning, which determines how large a survey needs to be to achieve a desired precision, is one of the most practical applications of this chapter.
The dataset for this unit contains 500 customer satisfaction survey responses, with variables covering channel, age group, satisfaction status, NPS score, and complaint history.
crumbs: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.3 Confidence interval for a proportion
Let \(\hat{p}\) be the sample proportion (the fraction of respondents with a given characteristic). The confidence interval for the population proportion \(p\) is:
\[\hat{p} \pm z^* \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\]
where \(z^*\) is the critical value from the standard normal distribution (1.96 for 95% confidence) and the term under the square root is the standard error of \(\hat{p}\).
This formula relies on the normal approximation to the binomial, which is reliable when \(n\hat{p} \geq 10\) and \(n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 10\).


Code
library(dplyr)

n_total   <- nrow(df)
n_satisfied <- sum(df$satisfied == "Yes")
p_hat     <- n_satisfied / n_total
se_p      <- sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / n_total)
z_star    <- qnorm(0.975)   # 95% CI

lower_95 <- p_hat - z_star * se_p
upper_95 <- p_hat + z_star * se_p

prop_ci <- data.frame(
  Statistic = c("n", "Satisfied (count)", "p-hat",
                "Standard error", "z* (95%)",
                "Lower 95% CI", "Upper 95% CI"),
  Value = round(c(n_total, n_satisfied, p_hat,
                  se_p, z_star, lower_95, upper_95), 4)
)

knitr::kable(prop_ci,
  col.names = c("Statistic", "Value"),
  caption   = "Table 19.1: 95% CI for customer satisfaction proportion") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"),
                             full_width = FALSE)

Statistic
Value

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.4 Using prop.test() in R


Code
result <- prop.test(n_satisfied, n_total, conf.level = 0.95,
                    correct = FALSE)

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.6 Sample size planning for proportions
Before collecting data, a researcher often needs to determine how large a sample is required to achieve a desired margin of error \(E\) at a given confidence level.
\[n = \left(\frac{z^*}{E}\right)^2 \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\]
When no prior estimate of \(p\) is available, the conservative choice \(\hat{p} = 0.5\) maximises the required sample size. This is conservative because \(p(1-p)\) is maximised at \(p = 0.5\).


Code
# Required sample size for various margins of error at 95% confidence
z_star <- 1.96
p_est  <- 0.5   # conservative

ss_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.10), function(E) {
  n_required <- ceiling((z_star / E)^2 * p_est * (1 - p_est))
  data.frame(
    Margin_of_error = paste0(E * 100, "%"),
    n_required      = n_required
  )
}))

knitr::kable(ss_table,
  col.names = c("Desired margin of error", "Required sample size (n)"),
  caption   = "Table 19.4: Sample size requirements (95% CI, p = 0.5)") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)



Table 19.4: Sample size requirements (95% CI, p = 0.5)


Desired margin of error
Required sample size (n)




1%
9604


2%
2401


3%
1068


5%
385


10%
97





A margin of error of ±3 percentage points at 95% confidence requires approximately 1,067 respondents. This is the basis for the “around 1,000 respondents” standard used in many national polls.

19.6.1 Using prior information to reduce required sample size


Code
# If prior data suggest p is close to 0.75, the required n is smaller
E      <- 0.03
p_prior <- 0.75

n_conservative <- ceiling((z_star / E)^2 * 0.5 * 0.5)
n_informed     <- ceiling((z_star / E)^2 * p_prior * (1 - p_prior))

prior_table <- data.frame(
  Approach = c("Conservative (p = 0.5)",
               paste0("Informed (p = ", p_prior, ")")),
  n_required = c(n_conservative, n_informed),
  Saving     = c(0, n_conservative - n_informed)
)

knitr::kable(prior_table,
  col.names = c("Approach", "Required n", "Savings vs conservative"),
  caption   = "Table 19.5: Sample size reduction with prior information") |>

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.7 Summary
A confidence interval for a proportion is \(\hat{p} \pm z^* \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}\), where \(z^*\) is the standard normal critical value. The interval estimates the population proportion and communicates the uncertainty from sampling.
Sample size planning uses the same formula in reverse: given a desired margin of error and confidence level, calculate the minimum n required. The conservative estimate \(\hat{p} = 0.5\) maximises the required sample size and is used when no prior information is available.
crumbs: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.8 Key terms



Table 19.6: Key terms for Chapter 19


Term
Definition




Proportion CI
A confidence interval for a population proportion: p-hat +/- z* sqrt(p-hat(1-p-hat)/n).


Standard error of p-hat
The estimated standard deviation of the sampling distribution of p-hat: sqrt(p-hat(1-p-hat)/n).


Margin of error
Half the width of a confidence interval; equal to z* times the standard error.


Sample size formula
The formula n = (z*/E)^2 * p-hat*(1-p-hat), giving the minimum sample size for a desired margin of error E.


Conservative estimate
Setting p-hat = 0.5 in the sample size formula to maximise n and ensure the required precision regardless of the true proportion.
crumbs: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning

title: 19  Chapter 19: Confidence Intervals for Proportions and Sample Size Planning
text:
19.9 Exercises

In a sample of 400 customers, 312 say they would recommend the company. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the population proportion and interpret the result.
Using Table 19.4, how many respondents are needed for a margin of error of ±2 percentage points at 95% confidence? What happens to the required sample size if the confidence level is raised to 99%?
A market research firm has previous data suggesting that approximately 65% of customers in a target segment prefer brand A. How many respondents are needed for a ±3% margin of error at 95% confidence? Compare this with the conservative estimate.
Using Figure 19.1, which channel has the widest confidence interval? What explains this? Does the channel with the widest CI necessarily have the lowest satisfaction rate?
A manager reads a report showing customer satisfaction at 74% with a margin of error of ±4 percentage points. She says: “So satisfaction is definitely above 70%.” Is she correct? Explain what the margin of error does and does not tell her.

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.1 Learning objectives
By the end of this chapter, the student should be able to:
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
State a null and alternative hypothesis for a given research question.
Explain the logic of hypothesis testing and what it means to reject the null.
Distinguish between Type I and Type II errors and give examples of each.
Explain the trade-off between Type I and Type II error rates.
Describe the consequences of each error type in a business context.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.2 Introduction
A confidence interval estimates a parameter and communicates uncertainty. Hypothesis testing asks a different question: is there sufficient evidence to reject a specific claim about a population?
This framework is used to evaluate almost every business decision that depends on data: whether a new product performs better than the existing one, whether a training programme improves performance, whether two market segments differ in their purchasing behaviour. The logic is simple but its implications are often misunderstood.
The dataset for this unit contains productivity records for 160 employees, half of whom received a new workflow tool (treatment group) and half of whom did not (control group). We use this to introduce the hypothesis testing framework before applying formal tests in Part 7.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.3 The hypothesis testing framework
Hypothesis testing begins with a question: does the observed data provide sufficient evidence against a specific claim? The claim being evaluated is the null hypothesis, written \(H_0\). The position that would be adopted if the null were rejected is the alternative hypothesis, written \(H_1\) or \(H_a\).
The null hypothesis always takes the form of no effect, no difference, or no relationship. It is the default position that will be maintained unless the data provide convincing evidence against it.



Table 20.1: Hypothesis pairs for common business questions


Research question
Null hypothesis H0
Alternative hypothesis H1




Did the workflow tool increase weekly tasks completed?
Mean tasks: treatment = control
Mean tasks: treatment > control


Does customer satisfaction differ between regions?
Mean satisfaction: all regions equal
Mean satisfaction: not all regions equal


Is the proportion of defective items below 5%?
Proportion defective >= 0.05
Proportion defective < 0.05


Do the two product versions have different conversion rates?
Conversion rate A = conversion rate B
Conversion rate A ≠ conversion rate B
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.4 The logic of hypothesis testing
Hypothesis testing does not prove the null hypothesis true or false. It assesses whether the observed data would be surprising if the null hypothesis were true.
The procedure is as follows:

State \(H_0\) and \(H_1\).
Collect data.
Compute a test statistic measuring how far the observed data lie from what \(H_0\) predicts.
Calculate the probability of observing a result this extreme (or more extreme) if \(H_0\) were true. This is the p-value.
If the p-value is below a pre-specified threshold \(\alpha\), reject \(H_0\).

The logic resembles a proof by contradiction: assume \(H_0\) is true, then ask whether the data are consistent with that assumption. If the data are highly unlikely under \(H_0\), the assumption is rejected.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
section: 20.6 Type I and Type II errors
text:
20.6 Type I and Type II errors
A hypothesis test can lead to two kinds of correct decision and two kinds of error.



Table 20.3: The four possible outcomes of a hypothesis test


Decision made
H0 is true (no effect)
H0 is false (real effect)




Reject H0
Type I error (false positive): alpha
Correct decision: 1 - beta (power)


Fail to reject H0
Correct decision: 1 - alpha
Type II error (false negative): beta





A Type I error (false positive) occurs when \(H_0\) is rejected even though it is true. The probability of this error is \(\alpha\), the significance level. Choosing \(\alpha = 0.05\) means that, in the long run, 5% of tests will incorrectly reject a true null hypothesis.
A Type II error (false negative) occurs when \(H_0\) is not rejected even though it is false. The probability of this error is \(\beta\). The power of a test is \(1 - \beta\): the probability of correctly detecting a real effect.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
section: 20.7 Business consequences of each error type
text:
20.7 Business consequences of each error type



Table 20.4: Business consequences of error types


Context
Type I consequence (false positive)
Type II consequence (false negative)




Workflow tool pilot
Roll out a tool that does not improve productivity. Wasted expenditure.
Fail to roll out a tool that genuinely improves productivity. Missed opportunity.


Quality control
Reject a good batch. Unnecessary waste and rework costs.
Accept a defective batch. Defective products reach customers.


Drug clinical trial
Approve a drug that is not effective. Patients receive no benefit; resources wasted.
Reject an effective drug. Patients denied a beneficial treatment.


A/B test: marketing campaign
Adopt a campaign that did not genuinely outperform. Budget misdirected.
Discard a campaign that genuinely outperformed. Revenue opportunity lost.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.8 The trade-off between error types
Reducing \(\alpha\) (the Type I error rate) increases \(\beta\) (the Type II error rate), for a fixed sample size. Making it harder to reject \(H_0\) reduces false positives but increases false negatives. The only way to reduce both simultaneously is to increase the sample size.



Table 20.5: Alpha levels and their implications


Alpha
Type I rate
Type II rate
Typical use




0.10
Higher
Lower
Exploratory research; low cost of false positives.


0.05
Standard
Standard
Standard scientific and business default.


0.01
Lower
Higher
High-stakes decisions; regulatory approval; patient safety.





The choice of \(\alpha\) should be made before data are collected and should reflect the relative costs of the two error types in the specific context.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.9 Worked example






Note



Scenario. The workflow tool pilot produced the results in Table 20.2. Before running a formal test, a manager wants to understand what errors are possible and what each would mean.





Table 20.6: Error scenarios for the workflow tool pilot


Decision
If H0 is true (no real effect)
If H0 is false (real effect)




Reject H0 (conclude tool helps)
Type I error: tool actually makes no difference, but we roll it out. Cost: implementation budget spent for no gain.
Correct decision: tool genuinely improves productivity by ~5.7 tasks/week. We deploy it.


Fail to reject H0 (conclude no evidence of effect)
Correct decision: we avoided a costly rollout of an ineffective tool.
Type II error: we miss a genuine improvement. Opportunity cost of not deploying.





The formal test — a two-sample t-test — is covered in Chapter 23. This chapter establishes the framework for interpreting whatever that test produces.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.10 Summary
Hypothesis testing evaluates whether observed data provide sufficient evidence to reject a null hypothesis. The null hypothesis \(H_0\) represents no effect or no difference; the alternative \(H_1\) represents the conclusion that would be adopted if \(H_0\) is rejected.
Two errors are possible: a Type I error (rejecting a true \(H_0\)) with probability \(\alpha\), and a Type II error (failing to reject a false \(H_0\)) with probability \(\beta\). These error rates are inversely related for a fixed sample size. The appropriate \(\alpha\) depends on the relative costs of each error type in the specific decision context.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.11 Key terms



Table 20.7: Key terms for Chapter 20


Term
Definition




Null hypothesis (H0)
The default claim of no effect, no difference, or no relationship; the hypothesis being tested.


Alternative hypothesis (H1)
The conclusion adopted if H0 is rejected; represents the research claim or expected effect.


Type I error
Rejecting H0 when it is in fact true; a false positive. Probability = alpha.


Type II error
Failing to reject H0 when it is in fact false; a false negative. Probability = beta.


Significance level (alpha)
The pre-specified maximum acceptable probability of a Type I error; typically 0.05.


Power (1 - beta)
The probability of correctly rejecting a false H0; equals 1 minus the Type II error rate.
crumbs: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors

title: 20  Chapter 20: Null and Alternative Hypotheses, Type I and Type II Errors
text:
20.12 Exercises

A pharmaceutical company tests a new drug. State \(H_0\) and \(H_1\). Which error type is more costly in this context, and how should this affect the choice of \(\alpha\)?
Using Table 20.2, what are the null and alternative hypotheses for comparing mean weekly tasks between treatment and control groups? Is this a one-tailed or two-tailed test?
A quality manager sets \(\alpha = 0.01\) for a test of whether a production line’s defect rate exceeds 3%. What does this mean in practice? What is the risk of setting \(\alpha\) this low?
A startup tests whether its new app feature increases daily active users. It sets \(\alpha = 0.10\). A competitor uses \(\alpha = 0.01\) for the same type of test. What does each company value more: avoiding false positives or avoiding false negatives?
Explain why increasing the sample size is the only way to simultaneously reduce both Type I and Type II error rates, while holding the other constant.

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.2 Introduction
Chapter 20 introduced the framework: null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, and error types. This chapter fills in the remaining concepts needed to interpret any hypothesis test: the direction of the test, the p-value that quantifies evidence, the effect size that measures magnitude, and the distinction between a statistically significant result and a practically important one.
The dataset for this unit contains results from an A/B test of two versions of a product checkout page. 300 users were randomly assigned to version A (current) or version B (redesigned). Variables include session duration, pages viewed, conversion rate, and order value.
crumbs: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.3 One-tailed versus two-tailed tests
The alternative hypothesis determines whether a test is one-tailed or two-tailed.
A two-tailed test is used when the research question is whether two groups differ, without specifying a direction: \[H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2\]
A one-tailed test is used when the research question specifies a direction: \[H_1: \mu_1 > \mu_2 \quad \text{or} \quad H_1: \mu_1 < \mu_2\]



Table 21.1: One-tailed versus two-tailed test selection


Test type
H1
Use when
Note




Two-tailed
mu1 ≠ mu2
Direction of difference is unknown or unspecified.
Default for most business tests; more conservative.


One-tailed (upper)
mu1 > mu2
Specifically hypothesising that group 1 is higher.
Should be specified before seeing data; not chosen post-hoc.


One-tailed (lower)
mu1 < mu2
Specifically hypothesising that group 1 is lower.
As above.





For the A/B test in this unit, the question is whether version B has a different conversion rate from version A — direction unspecified — so a two-tailed test is appropriate.
crumbs: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.4 The p-value
The p-value is the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme as the one computed, or more extreme, assuming \(H_0\) is true.
It is not the probability that \(H_0\) is true. It is not the probability of having made an error. It is a measure of how surprising the observed data are under the null hypothesis.
A small p-value means the observed result would be rare if \(H_0\) were true. A large p-value means the observed result is consistent with \(H_0\).

21.4.1 The decision rule
If \(p \leq \alpha\), reject \(H_0\). If \(p > \alpha\), fail to reject \(H_0\).


Code
library(dplyr)

ab_summary <- df |>
  group_by(version) |>
  summarise(
    n             = n(),
    conversion_rate = round(mean(converted == "Yes"), 3),
    mean_session    = round(mean(session_minutes), 2),
    mean_pages      = round(mean(pages_viewed), 2)
  )

knitr::kable(ab_summary,
  col.names = c("Version", "n", "Conversion rate",
                "Mean session (mins)", "Mean pages viewed"),
  caption   = "Table 21.2: A/B test descriptive summary") |>

                             full_width = FALSE)

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.5 Common p-value misinterpretations



Table 21.4: Common p-value misinterpretations


Incorrect statement
Correct interpretation




The p-value is the probability that H0 is true.
The p-value is the probability of observing a result this extreme if H0 were true. It says nothing about the probability that H0 is true.


A p-value of 0.04 means there is a 4% chance we are wrong.
The p-value is not an error probability for a single test. It is a long-run frequency statement.


A p-value > 0.05 proves H0 is true.
Failing to reject H0 means the data are consistent with H0, not that H0 is proved. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.


A p-value < 0.05 means the finding is important.
Statistical significance depends on sample size. A trivial effect can be statistically significant in a large sample. Practical importance requires separate assessment.
crumbs: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.6 Effect size
Statistical significance tells you whether an effect is detectable. Effect size tells you how large it is. A result can be statistically significant without being practically meaningful, particularly in large samples.
For the difference between two proportions, the simplest effect size is the absolute difference:
\[\text{Effect size} = \hat{p}_B - \hat{p}_A\]
For differences between means, Cohen’s d is commonly used:
\[d = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{\text{pooled}}}\]


Code
p_A   <- x_A / n_A
p_B   <- x_B / n_B
abs_diff <- p_B - p_A
rel_diff <- abs_diff / p_A

# Cohen's d for session minutes
m_A  <- mean(df$session_minutes[df$version == "A"])
m_B  <- mean(df$session_minutes[df$version == "B"])
s_p  <- sqrt(((n_A-1)*var(df$session_minutes[df$version=="A"]) +
               (n_B-1)*var(df$session_minutes[df$version=="B"])) /
              (n_A + n_B - 2))
cohens_d <- (m_B - m_A) / s_p

es_table <- data.frame(
  Measure     = c("Absolute difference in conversion (B - A)",
                  "Relative difference in conversion",
                  "Cohen's d for session minutes"),
  Value       = round(c(abs_diff, rel_diff, cohens_d), 3),
  Interpretation = c(
    paste0(round(abs_diff * 100, 1), " percentage points"),
    paste0(round(rel_diff * 100, 1), "% relative increase"),
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.2, "Negligible",
           ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.5, "Small",
                  ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.8, "Medium", "Large")))
  )
)

knitr::kable(es_table,
  col.names = c("Effect size measure", "Value", "Interpretation"),
  caption   = "Table 21.5: Effect size calculations for the A/B test") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.7 Statistical vs practical significance



Table 21.7: Statistical vs practical significance


Scenario
p-value verdict
Practical importance
Recommended action




Large sample, tiny effect
< 0.05 (significant)
Not meaningful
Report the effect size; question practical value.


Small sample, large effect
> 0.05 (not significant)
Potentially important but hard to detect
Increase sample size; do not discard the finding.


Large sample, large effect
< 0.05 (significant)
Both significant and meaningful
Strong evidence of an important effect.


Small sample, tiny effect
> 0.05 (not significant)
Neither significant nor meaningful
No basis for action from this test.

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.8 Power
The power of a test is the probability of correctly rejecting a false \(H_0\). Power is determined by three factors: sample size, effect size, and significance level \(\alpha\).
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta\]
A study with low power has a high risk of missing a real effect. Power analysis before data collection determines the sample size needed to detect a specified effect size with a specified probability.


Code
# Power for the conversion rate test
# Using pwr package if available, otherwise illustrative calculation
library(dplyr)
cat("Observed effect size (abs diff):", round(abs_diff, 3), "\n")


Observed effect size (abs diff): 0.113 


Code
cat("Sample size per group:", n_A, "\n")


Sample size per group: 150 


Code
# Approximate power using normal approximation
p_pool <- (x_A + x_B) / (n_A + n_B)
se_h0  <- sqrt(p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1/n_A + 1/n_B))
z_obs  <- abs_diff / se_h0
z_crit <- qnorm(0.975)
power_approx <- pnorm(z_obs - z_crit) + pnorm(-z_obs - z_crit)

cat("Approximate power:", round(power_approx, 3), "\n")


Approximate power: 0.593 


Code
cat("Interpretation: ~", round(power_approx * 100), "% chance of detecting",
    "\nthis effect size with n =", n_A, "per group at alpha = 0.05\n")


Interpretation: ~ 59 % chance of detecting 
this effect size with n = 150 per group at alpha = 0.05
crumbs: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.9 Summary
One-tailed tests specify a direction in \(H_1\); two-tailed tests do not. The p-value measures how surprising the data are under \(H_0\): a small p-value indicates the data would be unusual if \(H_0\) were true. It is not the probability that \(H_0\) is true.
Effect size quantifies the magnitude of an observed difference independently of sample size. Statistical significance (p < \(\alpha\)) indicates that an effect is detectable; practical significance requires a separate assessment of whether the effect is large enough to matter. Power is the probability of detecting a real effect; it is determined by sample size, effect size, and \(\alpha\).
crumbs: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

title: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance
text:
21.10 Key terms



Table 21.8: Key terms for Chapter 21


Term
Definition




Two-tailed test
A hypothesis test where H1 specifies a difference in either direction.


One-tailed test
A hypothesis test where H1 specifies the direction of the expected difference.


p-value
The probability of observing a result as extreme as the one obtained, or more extreme, assuming H0 is true.


Effect size
A standardised measure of the magnitude of an observed difference, independent of sample size.


Cohen's d
A standardised effect size for the difference between two means: (mean1 - mean2) / pooled SD.


Statistical significance
A result is statistically significant when p <= alpha; the evidence is sufficient to reject H0.


Practical significance
A result is practically significant when the effect is large enough to matter for real decisions.


Power
The probability of correctly rejecting a false H0; equals 1 - beta.
crumbs: 21  Chapter 21: One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests, Alpha, P-Values, Effect Size, Power, and Practical Significance

22.1 Learning objectives

title: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
text:
22.2 Introduction
Part 6 established the logic of hypothesis testing. Part 7 applies that logic to specific tests. This chapter covers the simplest case: comparing one sample mean to a known or hypothesised standard.
The dataset for this unit contains fill volume measurements for 60 bottles from a production line. The nominal fill target is 500 ml. Quality control requires that the mean fill does not differ significantly from this target.
crumbs: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test

title: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
section: 22.3 The z-test versus the one-sample t-test
text:
22.3 The z-test versus the one-sample t-test
Both tests evaluate whether a sample mean differs from a hypothesised value \(\mu_0\).
The z-test applies when the population standard deviation \(\sigma\) is known:
\[z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\]
The one-sample t-test applies when \(\sigma\) is unknown and must be estimated from the sample:
\[t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}\]
In practice, \(\sigma\) is almost never known. The one-sample t-test is therefore the standard tool. The z-test appears mainly in textbook problems and in cases where the sample is so large that the t-distribution and normal distribution are indistinguishable.
crumbs: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test

title: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
text:
22.4 Assumptions of the one-sample t-test

Independence: observations are independent of each other.
Approximate normality: the population is approximately normally distributed, or the sample is large enough for the CLT to apply (typically \(n \geq 30\)).

For the fill volume data, bottles are sampled independently and \(n = 60\), so the CLT ensures that the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal regardless of the population shape.
crumbs: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test

title: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
section: 22.7 Effect size: Cohen’s d for one sample
text:
22.7 Effect size: Cohen’s d for one sample
For a one-sample test, Cohen’s d measures how many standard deviations the sample mean lies from the hypothesised value:
\[d = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s}\]


Code
cohens_d <- (mean(df$fill_ml) - mu_0) / sd(df$fill_ml)
cat("Cohen's d =", round(cohens_d, 3), "\n")


Cohen's d = 0.044 


Code
cat("Interpretation:",
    ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.2, "Negligible",
           ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.5, "Small",
                  ifelse(abs(cohens_d) < 0.8, "Medium","Large"))), "\n")


Interpretation: Negligible 


A statistically significant result with a small effect size means the fill deficit, though detectable, may be too small to be operationally important. A result that is both significant and medium-to-large in effect size would warrant immediate action.
crumbs: 22  Chapter 22: Z-Tests and the One-Sample t-Test
